parts.content: Define simple harmonic motion (SHM).
parts.markscheme:
- Motion in which the acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position <marks>A1</marks>

- And is directed towards the equilibrium position (i.e., opposite to displacement) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identifying the Motion
steps.content:
To define Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), we look at the relationship between an object's acceleration and its position. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic C.1: Simple Harmonic Motion**. 

In the context of the question, the object is attached to a spring, which is a classic example of a system where the force (and therefore acceleration) changes depending on how far the spring is stretched or compressed.

steps.title: The Proportionality Rule
steps.content:
The first mark for this definition comes from describing the relationship between the magnitude of acceleration and the distance from the center. 

In SHM, the **acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement** from the equilibrium position. This means that if you pull the mass twice as far from the center ($0.20\text{ m}$ instead of $0.10\text{ m}$), the acceleration experienced by the mass will also double.

highlights.text: Motion in which the acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position

steps.title: The Direction Rule
steps.content:
The second mark requires you to specify the direction of this acceleration. 

The acceleration is **always directed towards the equilibrium position**. This is often referred to as a "restoring" effect because the acceleration always acts in the opposite direction to the displacement, trying to bring the object back to its resting point at the center.

highlights.text: And is directed towards the equilibrium position (i.e., opposite to displacement)

steps.title: Visualizing the Relationship
steps.content:
Mathematically, these two points are summarized by the defining equation for SHM:

$$a \propto -x$$

Where $a$ is acceleration and $x$ is displacement. The negative sign represents that the acceleration is in the opposite direction to the displacement (pointing back to equilibrium). We can visualize this relationship on a calculator by graphing acceleration against displacement.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Graphing menu.
2. Input the function f1(x) = -x.
3. Observe that the line passes through (0,0), showing proportionality, and has a negative gradient, showing the restoring direction.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = -X (this represents the proportional and opposite relationship).
3. Press [GRAPH].
4. Use [ZOOM] [Standard] to see the line passing through the origin with a negative slope.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = -x into the expression bar.
2. Observe the linear relationship where y (acceleration) is always the opposite sign of x (displacement).

calculator.description: Graphing the relationship between acceleration (y) and displacement (x) for SHM.

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure your definition includes both key components:

1. Acceleration is **directly proportional to the displacement** from equilibrium.
2. Acceleration is **always directed towards** that equilibrium position.

steps.title: Identify the governing equation
steps.content:
To define Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) using an equation-based approach, we start with the fundamental relationship that governs this motion. This is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. 

The defining equation for SHM is:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

steps.title: Analyze the proportionality
steps.content:
From the equation $$a = -\omega^2 x$$, we can see that since $\omega^2$ is a constant for the system, the acceleration $a$ is directly proportional to the displacement $x$ from the equilibrium position. 

In an exam, this is your first mark: 
- **Acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position.**

steps.title: Explain the negative sign
steps.content:
The negative sign in the equation is very important. It indicates that the acceleration $a$ is always in the opposite direction to the displacement $x$. 

If the object is displaced to the right (positive $x$), the acceleration acts to the left (negative $a$). This means the acceleration is always directed back toward the equilibrium position. This is often called a "restoring" effect.

steps.title: State the full definition
steps.content:
To earn both marks on an IB exam, you should combine these two insights into a formal statement:

Simple Harmonic Motion is motion where the **acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement** from the equilibrium position and is **directed towards the equilibrium position** (or opposite to the direction of displacement).

highlights.text: acceleration is directly proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position
directed towards the equilibrium position (i.e., opposite to displacement)

steps.title: Identify the restoring force
steps.content:
To define Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) using a force approach, we look at the **restoring force**. This is the resultant force that acts on the object to bring it back to its central equilibrium position. 

In this scenario, the restoring force is provided by the horizontal spring. This concept is found in **Topic C.1: Simple Harmonic Motion**.

steps.title: Relate force and displacement
steps.content:
The first requirement for SHM is that the magnitude of this restoring force $F$ must be **directly proportional** to the displacement $x$ from the equilibrium position. 

Mathematically, we express this as:
$$F \propto x$$

Because of Newton's Second Law ($$F=ma$$), if the force is proportional to the displacement, the acceleration must also be proportional to the displacement.

steps.title: Define the direction
steps.content:
The second requirement is the direction. The force must always act in the **opposite direction** to the displacement. This ensures the object is always being pushed or pulled back toward the equilibrium position.

We combine both requirements into one equation:
$$F = -kx$$

The negative sign indicates that the force (and therefore the acceleration) is always directed toward the equilibrium position.

steps.title: Final definition for marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks based on the IB markscheme, your definition should state:

1. The acceleration (or restoring force) is **directly proportional to the displacement** from the equilibrium position.
2. The acceleration (or restoring force) is **directed towards the equilibrium position** (or opposite to the direction of displacement).

parts.content: State the condition that must be satisfied for motion to be SHM.
parts.markscheme:
- State the condition: $a = - \omega^2 x$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the defining condition
steps.content: To determine if an object is undergoing Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), we look at the mathematical relationship between its acceleration and its displacement from the equilibrium position. This is a core concept covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

highlights.text: State the condition that must be satisfied for motion to be SHM.

steps.title: State the SHM equation
steps.content:
The mathematical definition of SHM is given by the equation:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

Where:
- $a$ is the acceleration of the object.
- $x$ is the displacement from the equilibrium position.
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency of the oscillation.

steps.title: Interpret the mathematical terms
steps.content:
This equation defines SHM because it shows two specific requirements:
1. **Proportionality**: Acceleration is directly proportional to displacement ($a \propto x$).
2. **Restoring Nature**: The negative sign indicates that acceleration is always directed towards the equilibrium position (opposite to the direction of displacement).

In an exam, stating the equation $a = -\omega^2 x$ is sufficient to earn the mark for the condition of SHM.

steps.title: Define SHM
steps.content: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) is a specific type of periodic oscillation. For a system to be performing SHM, its motion must follow a strict mathematical rule involving its acceleration and its position relative to a central point.

steps.title: State proportionality requirement
steps.content: The first requirement is that the magnitude of the object's acceleration must be **directly proportional** to its displacement from a fixed equilibrium position.

steps.title: Specify the direction
steps.content: The second requirement is that this acceleration must always be **directed toward the fixed equilibrium point**. This means the acceleration acts as a restoring force, always trying to pull the object back to its center position.

steps.title: State the final condition
steps.content:
Combining these into a single statement: the condition for Simple Harmonic Motion is that the **acceleration is proportional to the displacement from a fixed point and is always directed toward that point.**

This is represented by the formula:

$$\text{a} = -\omega^2 \text{x}$$

where $a$ is acceleration, $x$ is displacement, and the negative sign represents the direction towards the equilibrium point.

highlights.text: State the condition that must be satisfied for motion to be SHM.

steps.title: Define the Restoring Force
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the dynamics are governed by a **restoring force**. This force always acts in the opposite direction to the displacement, attempting to bring the object back to its equilibrium position. Mathematically, we say the force $F$ is proportional to the negative displacement $x$:

$$F \propto -x$$

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
From the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet, we know that net force is related to acceleration by Newton's Second Law:

$$F = ma$$

Since the mass $m$ of the object is constant, if the force is proportional to the negative displacement ($F \propto -x$), then the acceleration $a$ must also be proportional to the negative displacement ($a \propto -x$).

steps.title: State the SHM Condition
steps.content:
To satisfy the condition for SHM, the acceleration must be proportional to the displacement and directed towards the equilibrium position. We express this using the standard defining equation for SHM:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

Where:
- $a$ is the acceleration
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency (a constant)
- $x$ is the displacement from equilibrium

parts.content: Calculate the spring constant $k$ of the spring.
parts.markscheme:
- Formula: $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \Rightarrow k=\frac{4 \pi^2 m}{T^2}$

- Substitution: $k=\frac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.20}{(0.80)^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Evaluation: $\frac{7.90}{0.64}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Final Answer: $12.3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the period formula
steps.content:
To find the spring constant $k$, we start with the formula for the time period $T$ of a mass-spring system undergoing Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM). This concept is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. 

The formula is:

$$T = 2	ext{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for spring constant
steps.content:
Since we need to calculate $k$, we rearrange the formula by squaring both sides to remove the square root:

$$\begin{aligned} T^2 &= 4\pi^2 \left( \frac{m}{k} \right) \\ k &= \frac{4\pi^2 m}{T^2} \end{aligned}$$

This gives us a direct way to solve for $k$ using the mass and period provided in the question.

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values from the problem: the mass $m = 0.20 \text{ kg}$ and the period $T = 0.80 \text{ s}$. 

$$k = \frac{4\pi^2 \times 0.20}{(0.80)^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate the expression to find the value of $k$. According to the markscheme, we award marks for the substitution and the intermediate evaluation steps.

$$\begin{aligned} k &= \frac{4\pi^2 \times 0.20}{0.64} \\ k &\approx \frac{7.90}{0.64} \\ k &= 12.3 \text{ N/m} \end{aligned}$$

Don't forget the units for the spring constant, which are Newtons per meter ($\text{N/m}$).

gdc.instructions:
1. In the run-matrix menu, input the fraction (4 * π^2 * 0.20) / (0.80^2).
2. Press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression k = (4 * pi^2 * 0.2) / (0.8^2) into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the spring constant k directly using the rearranged period formula.

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
First, we identify the values provided in the question. We are given the mass ($m$) and the time period ($T$) of the oscillation.

- Mass $m = 0.20 \text{ kg}$
- Period $T = 0.80 \text{ s}$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the angular frequency $\omega$ is related to the period $T$ by the formula:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substituting our value for $T$:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{0.80} \approx 7.854 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / 0.80

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency (omega).

steps.title: Relate frequency to $k$
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system, the angular frequency is determined by the mass $m$ and the spring constant $k$ through the relationship $\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m}$.

Rearranging this to solve for $k$ gives us:

$$k = m\omega^2$$

steps.title: Determine spring constant
steps.content:
Now, substitute the mass and the calculated angular frequency into the rearranged equation:

$$\begin{aligned} k &= 0.20 \times (7.854)^2 \\ k &\approx 12.337 \text{ N m}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (matching the given values), we get $k = 12 \text{ N m}^{-1}$, or more precisely $12.3 \text{ N m}^{-1}$ for three marks as per the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.20 by the square of the previous result.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 0.20 by the square of the previous result.

desmos.instructions: Multiply 0.20 by the square of the previous result.

calculator.description: Calculate the final spring constant k.

parts.content: Determine the maximum speed of the object during the motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate angular frequency: $\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{0.80}=7.85 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Use formula for max speed: $v_{\max }=A \omega$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Final Answer: $v_{\max }=0.10 \times 7.85=0.785 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To find the maximum speed, we first need to identify the amplitude ($A$) and the period ($T$) of the oscillation from the question.

*   The amplitude is the maximum displacement from equilibrium: $A = 0.10 \text{ m}$
*   The period is the time for one full cycle: $T = 0.80 \text{ s}$

highlights.text: pulled 0.10 m from its equilibrium position

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), we often use angular frequency ($\omega$) to describe how fast the system oscillates. We can calculate this using the period $T$:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substituting our value for the period:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{0.80} \approx 7.85 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / 0.80

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency using the period.

steps.title: Determine maximum speed
steps.content:
The maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$) in SHM occurs as the object passes through the equilibrium position. It is calculated by multiplying the angular frequency by the amplitude:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega A$$

Using our values:

$$v_{\text{max}} = 7.85398... \times 0.10$$
$$v_{\text{max}} \approx 0.785 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by 0.10: Ans * 0.10

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum speed by multiplying the angular frequency by the amplitude.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The maximum speed of the object during the motion is $0.785 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

In the IB markscheme, you earn:
- **1 mark** for correctly calculating $\omega$.
- **1 mark** for using the correct formula for $v_{\text{max}}$.
- **1 mark** for the final answer with correct units.

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
First, we list the values given in the problem. The object has a mass $m$, an initial displacement which corresponds to the amplitude $A$, and a period $T$.

- Mass: $m = 0.20\text{ kg}$
- Amplitude: $A = 0.10\text{ m}$
- Period: $T = 0.80\text{ s}$

This system undergoes Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), as covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Determine the spring constant
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system, the period $T$ is related to the mass $m$ and the spring constant $k$ by the formula:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

To find $k$, we rearrange the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} T^2 &= 4\pi^2 \frac{m}{k} \\ k &= \frac{4\pi^2 m}{T^2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$k = \frac{4\pi^2(0.20)}{(0.80)^2} \approx 12.337 \text{ N m}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the spring constant k.

steps.title: Apply conservation of energy
steps.content:
In SHM on a frictionless surface, total mechanical energy is conserved. 

At the maximum displacement ($x = A$), the velocity is zero, so all energy is stored as elastic potential energy:
$$E_{p, \text{max}} = \frac{1}{2} k A^2$$

At the equilibrium position ($x = 0$), the potential energy is zero, and the object moves at its maximum speed $v_{\text{max}}$. All energy is now kinetic:
$$E_{k, \text{max}} = \frac{1}{2} m v_{\text{max}}^2$$

Equating these two states:
$$\frac{1}{2} k A^2 = \frac{1}{2} m v_{\text{max}}^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the maximum speed
steps.content:
Now we solve for $v_{\text{max}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m v_{\text{max}}^2 &= k A^2 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= \sqrt{\frac{k A^2}{m}} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting our values:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{12.337 \times (0.10)^2}{0.20}}$$

$$v_{\text{max}} \approx 0.785 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum speed using the derived formula.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The maximum speed of the object is $0.785 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

**Marks Breakdown:**
- **A1**: Correct angular frequency $\omega$ (or equivalent step in finding $k$).
- **M1**: Correct use of an appropriate energy or speed formula.
- **A1**: Correct final value with units.

options.markscheme: Using $U=\tfrac12kx^2$ but incorrectly taking $E=U$. Correct method: $k=m\omega^2$ and $E=K+U=3U$.
Using $K=2U$ but incorrectly taking $E=K$. Correct method: $E=K+U=3U$.
$k=m\omega^2=0.20\times 10^2=20\ \text{N m}^{-1}$. $U=\tfrac12kx^2=\tfrac12\times 20\times (0.10)^2=0.10\ \text{J}$. Given $K=2U$, total $E=K+U=3U=0.30\ \text{J}$.
Likely arithmetic error (e.g. misplacing a decimal when evaluating $(0.10)^2$ or multiplying by $k$). Correct total energy is $0.30\ \text{J}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires understanding SHM phase relationships between displacement, velocity, and energy to choose the correct graph.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. In SHM the speed changes with time, so kinetic energy is not constant.

options.content: A sine wave in the same phase as displacement
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Kinetic energy depends on $v^2$, not on displacement, and is always non-negative.

options.content: A sine wave out of phase with displacement by $\pi/2$
options.markscheme: Incorrect. While velocity is $\pi/2$ out of phase with displacement, kinetic energy is proportional to $v^2$ and so is not sinusoidal (and does not take negative values).

options.content: A cosine-squared wave with twice the frequency of displacement
options.markscheme: Correct. For SHM, $x=A\cos(\omega t)$ so $v=-A\omega\sin(\omega t)$ and $K=\tfrac12 mv^2\propto \sin^2(\omega t)=\tfrac12[1-\cos(2\omega t)]$, i.e. always non-negative and with frequency $2\omega$.

questions.specification: A simple pendulum with a length of $0.8 \text{ m}$ is used in a physics experiment to demonstrate oscillatory motion near the surface of Earth.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Both parts ask for the period of a simple pendulum and how it changes when the length is doubled, directly testing the time period relationship for oscillatory systems (SHM).

parts.content: Calculate the period of this pendulum.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula for the period of a pendulum: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values and calculate: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{0.8}{9.81}} = 1.79 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To solve this, we first need to identify the values provided in the question and the constants we'll need from our data booklet. We are given the length of the pendulum and we need the value for acceleration due to gravity on Earth.

- Length ($L$) = $0.8 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) $\approx 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ as per the data booklet)

steps.title: Select the period formula
steps.content:
This question falls under **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. For a simple pendulum undergoing small oscillations, the relationship between its length and its orbital period is given by a specific formula found in your data booklet under the section for oscillatory systems:

$$\begin{aligned} T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $T$ is the period (seconds)
- $L$ is the length (meters)
- $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we simply substitute our known values into the formula. To match the precision often expected in IB markschemes ($1.79 \text{ s}$), we will use $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{0.8}{9.81}} \\ T &\approx 1.79428... \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

If you use $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ from the constants list, you get $T \approx 1.80 \text{ s}$. Both are generally accepted, but $1.79 \text{ s}$ is the standard answer here.

steps.title: Calculator verification
steps.content: Using a calculator is the most reliable way to handle the square root and the factor of $2\pi$. Follow these steps to get the correct result.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the expression directly into the main calculation screen.
2. Use the square root symbol and parentheses to ensure the fraction is entirely under the root.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [^] to get π.
2. Type: 2 * π * √(0.8 / 9.81)
3. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type the expression as written. Desmos will automatically format the square root and pi.

calculator.description: Calculate the period using the pendulum formula.

steps.title: Final answer and rounding
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you round your final answer to an appropriate number of significant figures (usually 3 in IB Physics) and include the correct units.

$$T = 1.79 \text{ s}$$

According to the markscheme, you get 1 mark for stating the correct formula and 1 mark for the final calculated value.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this using the angular frequency approach, we first list the known values. The length of the pendulum $L$ is given, and we can find the acceleration due to gravity $g$ from the physical constants in your data booklet.

*   Length ($L$) = $0.8 \text{ m}$
*   Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (using the standard value for calculations to match the markscheme)

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion (Topic C.1), the angular frequency $\omega$ for a simple pendulum is related to its length and gravity by the formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.8}} \\ \omega &\approx 3.50 \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(9.81/0.8) in the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(9.81/0.8) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency omega.

steps.title: Solve for the period
steps.content:
Now that we have the angular frequency, we can find the period $T$. The relationship between period and angular frequency is defined as:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Using our calculated $\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{2\pi}{3.50...} \\ T &\approx 1.79 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2*pi / Ans to use the full precision of the previous result.

calculator.description: Calculate the period T using the precise value of omega.

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
The final period of the pendulum is **1.79 s** (rounded to 3 significant figures).

**Marking Breakdown:**
*   **M1:** Correct formula used (implicitly through $\omega$ and $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ or by stating $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$).
*   **A1:** Correct substitution and final numerical answer.

parts.content: If the length of the pendulum is doubled, determine the new period.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to calculate with doubled length $L = 1.6 \text{ m}$: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1.6}{9.81}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct answer: $T = 2.54 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept 2.53 s from follow-through ($\sqrt{2} \times 1.79$).</note>

steps.title: Calculate the new length
steps.content:
The question states that the original length of the pendulum is $0.8 \text{ m}$ and that for this part of the experiment, the length is doubled.

$$\begin{aligned} L_{new} &= 0.8 \text{ m} \times 2 \\ L_{new} &= 1.6 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: length of the pendulum is doubled

steps.title: State the period formula
steps.content:
To find the time period of a simple pendulum, we use the formula from **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

From the **Physical Constants** table in your data booklet, we know that the acceleration due to gravity on Earth is:

$$g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the new length $L = 1.6 \text{ m}$ and the constant $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ directly into the equation to calculate the new period $T$:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1.6}{9.8}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * π * sqrt(1.6 / 9.8) into the main calculation screen and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] (for π), then [2nd] [x²] (for √). Type 1.6 / 9.8 and close the parentheses. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * pi * sqrt(1.6 / 9.8) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the period using the direct substitution values.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
Evaluating the expression gives:

$$T \approx 2.54 \text{ s}$$

This accounts for both marks: one for the correct substitution of the doubled length ($1.6 \text{ m}$) into the formula, and one for the final numerical answer.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
In IB Physics **Topic C.1.2**, we define the time period ($T$) of a simple pendulum. The fundamental relationship shows that the period is directly proportional to the square root of its length ($L$):

$$T \propto \sqrt{L}$$

This means that if we change the length, the period will change by the square root of that same factor. Specifically, if the length is doubled, the period increases by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.

steps.title: Calculate the initial period
steps.content:
First, we calculate the period of the pendulum with its original length of $0.8 \text{ m}$ using the formula for a simple pendulum:

$$T_1 = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L_1}{g}}$$

Substituting $L_1 = 0.8 \text{ m}$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$T_1 = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.8}{9.81}} \approx 1.794 \text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi * sqrt(0.8 / 9.81) and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] (for pi). Press [2nd] [x²] (for sqrt). Enter 0.8 / 9.81. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * pi * sqrt(0.8 / 9.81) into the expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial period T1.

steps.title: Apply the proportionality factor
steps.content:
The question states the length is doubled ($L_2 = 2L_1$). Using our proportionality relationship, the new period $T_2$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} T_2 &= T_1 \times \sqrt{2} \\ T_2 &= 1.794 \times \sqrt{2} \\ T_2 &\approx 2.537 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This method is efficient because it directly utilizes the square root relationship without needing to re-calculate the entire formula from scratch.

gdc.instructions: Enter 1.794 * sqrt(2) and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type 1.794 * sqrt(2) into the expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the new period by multiplying the initial period by the square root of 2.

highlights.text: length of the pendulum is doubled

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to a standard three significant figures, we get:

$$T = 2.54 \text{ s}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct substitution (or identification of the $\sqrt{2}$ factor) and one mark for the final numerical answer.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the defining SHM relationship a(t) = −ω²x(t) to determine the angular frequency from given acceleration and displacement (core SHM analysis).

options.markscheme: Using $a=-\omega^2x$ with $a=3.0$ and $x=-0.12$: $3.0=-\omega^2(-0.12)=0.12\omega^2\Rightarrow \omega^2=25\text{ s}^{-2}\Rightarrow \omega=5.0\text{ rad s}^{-1}$.
This corresponds to incorrectly using $\omega^2=0.12/3.0$ (inverting the ratio). Correct method gives $\omega=5.0\text{ rad s}^{-1}$.
This could result from substituting $v$ into an incorrect relation such as $a=-\omega v$. In SHM, the correct relation at an instant is $a=-\omega^2 x$, giving $\omega=5.0\text{ rad s}^{-1}$.
This is $\omega^2$ rather than $\omega$. From $\omega^2=25\text{ s}^{-2}$, the angular frequency is $\omega=5.0\text{ rad s}^{-1}$.

parts.content: Determine the total energy of the system.
parts.markscheme:
The total energy of the system consists of the elastic potential energy stored in the spring and the kinetic energy of the object:

$$E_\text{total} = E_\text{elastic} + E_\text{kinetic}$$

- State the formula for elastic potential energy: $E_\text{elastic} = \frac{1}{2}kx_0^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute the given values to find elastic potential energy: $E_\text{elastic} = \frac{1}{2}(50)(0.05)^2 = 0.0625\,\text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

Since the object is initially at rest, its kinetic energy is zero ($E_\text{kinetic} = 0\,\text{J}$).

- Calculate the total energy of the system: $E_\text{total} = 0.0625\,\text{J} + 0\,\text{J} = 0.0625\,\text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award M1 for using the correct formula for elastic potential energy. Award A1 for substituting the correct values and evaluating the elastic potential energy correctly. Award A1 for correctly calculating the total energy by adding the elastic potential energy and kinetic energy.</note>

steps.title: Identify system parameters
steps.content:
To find the total energy, let's first list the information given in the problem:
- Mass of the object: $m = 0.2 \, \text{kg}$
- Spring constant: $k = 50 \, \text{N/m}$
- Displacement from equilibrium: $x = 0.05 \, \text{m}$

This system involves **Topic A.3 (Work, energy and power)** and **Topic C.1 (Simple harmonic motion)**, where we look at how energy transfers between potential and kinetic forms.

steps.title: Analyze the energy state
steps.content:
The total energy ($E_{\text{total}}$) of a mass-spring system is the sum of its kinetic energy ($E_k$) and elastic potential energy ($E_p$):

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_k + E_p$$

When the object is displaced by $0.05 \, \text{m}$ and held there (or at the moment it reaches its maximum displacement), it is **momentarily at rest**. Since the velocity $v = 0$, the kinetic energy is also zero:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = 0 \, \text{J}$$

Therefore, at this specific point, the total energy of the system is equal to the elastic potential energy stored in the spring.

steps.title: Calculate elastic potential energy
steps.content:
We use the formula for elastic potential energy. Although not listed in the snippet provided, it is a fundamental equation in IB Physics for springs:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \, \text{N/m} \times (0.05 \, \text{m})^2 \\ E_p &= 25 \times 0.0025 \\ E_p &= 0.0625 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, stating the formula correctly earns you **[1 mark]** (M1), and the correct substitution and calculation earns you another **[1 mark]** (A1).

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 50 * 0.05^2 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a cell to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the elastic potential energy using the given k and x.

steps.title: Determine total energy
steps.content:
Since the kinetic energy is zero at the point of maximum displacement, the total energy is simply the elastic potential energy we just calculated:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 0.0625 \, \text{J} + 0 \, \text{J} = 0.0625 \, \text{J}$$

Final answer: The total energy of the system is $0.0625 \, \text{J}$. Identifying the total energy earns the final **[1 mark]** (A1).

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To find the total energy using the Work-Energy Principle, we first list the known values for the spring-mass system:

- Mass of the object: $m = 0.2 \, \text{kg}$
- Spring constant: $k = 50 \, \text{N/m}$
- Displacement from equilibrium: $x = 0.05 \, \text{m}$

In this approach, the total energy of the system is equal to the work done to displace the object from its equilibrium position.

steps.title: Calculate the maximum force
steps.content:
According to Hooke's Law, the force $F$ exerted by a spring is proportional to its extension $x$. We calculate the maximum force required to reach the displacement of $0.05 \, \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} F &= kx \\ F &= (50 \, \text{N/m})(0.05 \, \text{m}) \\ F &= 2.5 \, \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

This represents the "height" of our force-extension graph at the maximum displacement.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, type 50 * 0.05 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 50 * 0.05 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum force using Hooke's Law.

steps.title: Determine work done (Energy)
steps.content:
The work done to displace the spring is the area under the force-extension ($F$-$x$) graph. Since $F$ varies linearly with $x$, the area is a triangle with base $x$ and height $F$:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our values into the formula for work (which equals the total energy $E_{\text{total}}$):

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{total}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot F \\ E_{\text{total}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot (0.05 \, \text{m}) \cdot (2.5 \, \text{N}) \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, type 0.5 * 0.05 * 2.5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.05 * 2.5 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy (work done) by finding the area of the triangle.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Completing the calculation gives us the total energy stored in the system:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 0.0625 \, \text{J}$$

This total energy remains constant as the object oscillates (assuming no energy is lost to the surroundings), which aligns with the principles of conservation of energy covered in **Topic A.3.1**.

steps.title: Define the energy approach
steps.content:
In a Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) system, energy is conserved and oscillates between potential and kinetic forms. The **total energy** of the system is equal to the **maximum kinetic energy** ($E_{k, max}$), which occurs when the object passes through the equilibrium position at its maximum velocity ($v_{max}$).

To find this, we first need to determine the angular frequency ($\omega$) of the system using the mass and the spring constant.

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
The angular frequency $\omega$ for a mass-spring system is given by the relationship:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Given:
- $k = 50 \, \text{N/m}$
- $m = 0.2 \, \text{kg}$

Substituting these values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.2}} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root of (50 divided by 0.2).

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(50/0.2) into the expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency.

steps.title: Find maximum velocity
steps.content:
Now we can find the maximum velocity ($v_{max}$) of the object. In SHM, the maximum velocity is related to the amplitude ($x_0$) and angular frequency by:

$$v_{max} = \omega x_0$$

Given the displacement (amplitude) is $0.05 \, \text{m}$:

$$v_{max} = 15.81 \times 0.05 \approx 0.791 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the result for ω by 0.05.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum velocity.

steps.title: Determine total energy
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the total energy as the maximum kinetic energy using the formula from the data booklet:

$$E_{total} = E_{k, max} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{max}^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}(0.2)(0.791)^2$$

$$E_{total} = 0.1 \times 0.625 = 0.0625 \, \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 0.5 times the mass times the square of the maximum velocity.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The total energy of the system is **$0.0625 \, \text{J}$**.

In an IB exam, marks are awarded for:
- Correctly identifying the energy formula (M1)
- Correct substitution of values (A1)
- Final correct answer with units (A1)

parts.content: Calculate the kinetic and potential energy when the object is halfway through its maximum displacement.
parts.markscheme:
- State the total energy calculated in Part 1: $E_{total} = 0.0625\,\text{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

At the halfway position, the displacement is $x = 0.025\,\text{m}$.

- Calculate the potential energy at this position: $E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}(50)(0.025)^2 = 0.015625\,\text{J} \approx 0.016\,\text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate the kinetic energy by subtracting potential from total energy: $E_k = E_{total} - E_p = 0.0625 - 0.015625 = 0.046875\,\text{J} \approx 0.047\,\text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award M1 for using the correct total energy. Award A1 for correct calculation of potential energy. Award A1 for correct calculation of kinetic energy (allow follow through from potential energy calculation).</note>

steps.title: Recall the total energy
steps.content:
In an ideal oscillating system like this robotic arm's spring, the total energy ($E_{\text{total}}$) remains constant throughout the motion. From Part 1, we know the total energy calculated at the maximum displacement is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 0.0625 \, \text{J}$$

steps.title: Find the halfway displacement
steps.content:
The question asks for the energy when the object is halfway through its maximum displacement. Since the maximum displacement is $0.05 \, \text{m}$, we divide this by 2 to find our specific position $x$:

$$x = \frac{0.05 \, \text{m}}{2} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the potential energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the elastic potential energy ($E_p$) at this halfway point using the spring constant $k = 50 \, \text{N/m}$ and the displacement $x = 0.025 \, \text{m}$:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}(50)(0.025)^2 = 0.015625 \, \text{J}$$

Rounded to two significant figures (as per the inputs), we get $E_p \approx 0.016 \, \text{J}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 50 * 0.025^2 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the potential energy value.

steps.title: Determine the kinetic energy
steps.content:
Using the conservation of energy, the total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy ($E_{\text{total}} = E_k + E_p$). We can find the kinetic energy ($E_k$) by subtracting the potential energy from the total energy:

$$E_k = E_{\text{total}} - E_p$$

$$E_k = 0.0625 - 0.015625 = 0.046875 \, \text{J}$$

Rounded to two significant figures, we get $E_k \approx 0.047 \, \text{J}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.0625 - 0.015625 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Subtract potential energy from total energy to find kinetic energy.

steps.title: Identify halfway displacement
steps.content:
The question specifies the object is halfway through its maximum displacement. Given the displacement is $0.05 \, \text{m}$, we calculate $x$:

$$x = \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
Using the properties of a spring-mass system in SHM, we find the angular frequency $\omega$ using the spring constant ($k$) and mass ($m$):

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.2}} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(50/0.2)

calculator.description: Calculate angular frequency

steps.title: Find velocity using SHM
steps.content:
We use the SHM kinematic equation $v = \omega\sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$ to find the velocity at the halfway point:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{250} \times \sqrt{0.05^2 - 0.025^2} \\ v &= \sqrt{250} \times \sqrt{0.001875} \\ v &\approx 0.685 \, \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate sqrt(250) * sqrt(0.05^2 - 0.025^2)

calculator.description: Calculate velocity at x = 0.025

steps.title: Calculate kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now, apply the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k} &= \frac{1}{2}mv^{2} \\ E_{k} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.2)(0.6846...)^2 \\ E_{k} &= 0.046875 \, \text{J} \approx 0.047 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate kinetic energy

steps.title: Calculate potential energy
steps.content:
Finally, calculate the potential energy at the same position $x = 0.025 \, \text{m}$ using $E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{p} &= \frac{1}{2}(50)(0.025)^2 \\ E_{p} &= 0.015625 \, \text{J} \approx 0.016 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate potential energy

steps.title: Recall the total energy
steps.content:
First, we need the total energy of the system from Part 1. In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), total energy remains constant if there is no friction. 

$$E_{\text{total}} = 0.0625 \, \text{J}$$

steps.title: Understand the energy proportions
steps.content:
The potential energy ($E_p$) of a mass-spring system is given by the formula:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

This shows that potential energy is **directly proportional to the square of the displacement** ($E_p \propto x^2$). Since total energy ($E_T$) is the potential energy at maximum displacement ($A$), we can say:

$$E_p = E_T \left(\frac{x}{A}\right)^2$$

steps.title: Calculate potential energy ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the energy when the object is "halfway through its maximum displacement." This means $x = \frac{1}{2}A$.

Substituting this into our proportion:

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= E_T \left(\frac{0.5A}{A}\right)^2 \\ E_p &= E_T (0.5)^2 \\ E_p &= \frac{1}{4}E_T \end{aligned}$$

At the halfway point, the potential energy is exactly **one-fourth** of the total energy.

steps.title: Find numerical potential energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the value of $E_p$ using the total energy from Step 0:

$$E_p = 0.25 \times 0.0625 \, \text{J} = 0.015625 \, \text{J}$$

Rounding to two significant figures as per the markscheme, we get:

$$E_p \approx 0.016 \, \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.25 by 0.0625.

calculator.description: Calculate 1/4 of the total energy.

steps.title: Determine kinetic energy ratio
steps.content:
By the law of conservation of energy, total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy:

$$E_T = E_k + E_p$$

If $E_p$ is $\frac{1}{4}$ of the total energy, then the kinetic energy ($E_k$) must be the remaining **three-fourths**:

$$E_k = E_T - \frac{1}{4}E_T = \frac{3}{4}E_T$$

steps.title: Calculate numerical kinetic energy
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the value of $E_k$:

$$E_k = 0.75 \times 0.0625 \, \text{J} = 0.046875 \, \text{J}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get:

$$E_k \approx 0.047 \, \text{J}$$

This completes the calculation for both energy types at the halfway position!

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.75 by 0.0625.

calculator.description: Calculate 3/4 of the total energy.

parts.content: Describe how the energy transforms during one cycle of oscillation.
parts.markscheme:
- State energy at maximum displacement: At maximum displacement, all energy is potential <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe transformation towards equilibrium: As it moves towards equilibrium, potential energy decreases and kinetic energy increases <marks>A1</marks>
- State energy at equilibrium: At equilibrium, all energy is kinetic <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe transformation away from equilibrium: As it moves away from equilibrium, kinetic energy decreases and potential energy increases <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Start at maximum displacement
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), we look at how energy moves between different forms. At the very start, the object is **displaced $0.05 \, \text{m}$ from equilibrium**. At this point, the object is momentarily at rest before it begins to move. 

Because its velocity is zero, its kinetic energy ($E_k$) is zero. All of the system's energy is stored as **elastic potential energy** ($E_p$) in the spring.

steps.title: Movement towards equilibrium
steps.content:
As the object is released and moves towards the equilibrium position, the restoring force of the spring causes it to accelerate. 

During this phase, the displacement decreases, which means the **potential energy decreases**. According to the law of conservation of energy (found in **Topic A.3.1**), this energy must go somewhere: it is transformed into **kinetic energy**, and the object speeds up.

steps.title: Energy at equilibrium
steps.content:
When the object reaches the equilibrium position ($x = 0$), the spring is neither stretched nor compressed. At this exact moment, the elastic potential energy is zero. 

Since energy is conserved, the object is now at its maximum speed. All of the system's energy is now **kinetic energy**.

steps.title: Movement away from equilibrium
steps.content:
As the object's inertia carries it past the equilibrium point, it begins to compress (or stretch) the spring in the opposite direction. 

The spring now exerts a force that slows the object down. In this phase, the **kinetic energy decreases** as the object slows, and the energy is transformed back into **elastic potential energy** until it reaches the opposite maximum displacement and the cycle repeats.

steps.title: Calculate Maximum Potential Energy
steps.content:
To analyze the energy cycle quantitatively, we first calculate the total energy in the system. At the maximum displacement (amplitude), the object is momentarily at rest, so all the energy is stored as **elastic potential energy** ($E_p$).

Using the formula for elastic potential energy:
$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= \frac{1}{2}(50)(0.05)^2 \\ E_p &= 0.0625 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

At this point, the kinetic energy ($E_k$) is $0 \text{ J}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the result on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy at maximum displacement.

steps.title: Describe Transformation Toward Equilibrium
steps.content:
As the object moves from the maximum displacement position toward the equilibrium position, the displacement ($x$) decreases. 

This causes the **elastic potential energy to decrease**. According to the law of conservation of energy (Topic A.3.1), this energy is not lost; it is transformed into **kinetic energy**, causing the object to speed up.

steps.title: Analyze the Equilibrium Position
steps.content:
When the object reaches the equilibrium position ($x = 0$):
- The elastic potential energy is $0 \text{ J}$ because the spring is no longer stretched or compressed.
- All the system's energy has been converted into **kinetic energy**.

At this point, $E_k = 0.0625 \text{ J}$, and the object is moving at its maximum speed.

steps.title: Describe Transformation Away From Equilibrium
steps.content:
As the object continues past the equilibrium position, it begins to compress (or stretch) the spring again. 

As the displacement increases in the opposite direction, the **kinetic energy decreases** and is transformed back into **elastic potential energy**. This process continues until the object reaches the opposite maximum displacement, where $E_k = 0 \text{ J}$ and $E_p = 0.0625 \text{ J}$, completing half a cycle. The process then reverses to complete the full oscillation.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This value is obtained if the displacement in cm is not converted to meters before taking the square root: $\sqrt{\frac{8.0}{2.0}} = 2.0$.
Incorrect. This may result from a simple division error ($8/2$) without applying the square root.

Correct. Using the defining equation for SHM, $a = -\omega^2 x$, we find $\omega^2 = \frac{|a|}{|x|}$. Converting displacement to meters: $\omega^2 = \frac{8.0 \text{ m s}^{-2}}{0.02 \text{ m}} = 400 \text{ s}^{-2}$. Thus, $\omega = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$.

parts.content: Determine the speed of the object when the displacement is $x = 0.040\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
$v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{(0.060)^2 - (0.040)^2} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{0.0036 - 0.0016} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{0.002} = 0.894\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
 Correct formula and substitution 1 mark , correct final answer with unit 1 mark

parts.content: Calculate the total mechanical energy of the object.
parts.markscheme:
$E_T = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x_0^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.060^2 = 0.072\ \text{J}$
 Correct substitution 1 mark , correct final answer with unit 1 mark

parts.content: Determine the time it takes for the object to move from $x = 0.040\ \text{m}$ to $x = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
At $x = 0.040$, $\sin(\theta) = \frac{0.040}{0.060} = \frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \theta = \sin^{-1}(2/3) = 0.7297$
 $\omega t = 0.7297 \Rightarrow t = \frac{0.7297}{20} = 0.0365\ \text{s}$
 From $x = 0.040$ to $x = 0$, this corresponds to one quarter of a cycle from that point:
 $t = \frac{0.7297}{20} - \frac{\pi}{2}/20 = 0.0365 - 0.0785 = -0.042\ \text{s}$ (take absolute value)
 or more simply:
 $\Delta t = \frac{1}{\omega} \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{x}{x_0} \right) = \frac{1}{20} \cdot \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{2}{3} \right) = 0.0365\ \text{s}$
 1 mark for correct approach, 1 mark for final time with units

parts.content: Calculate the acceleration of the object when $x = 0.040\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
$a = -\omega^2 x = -400 \cdot 0.040 = -16.0\ \text{m s}^{-2}$
 Magnitude: $16.0\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ 1 mark

parts.content: Sketch an energy vs. displacement graph, indicating both kinetic and potential energy variations.
parts.markscheme:
Sketch:
Total energy $E_T = 0.072\ \text{J}$, horizontal line

$E_p = \frac{1}{2}k x^2$ curve, parabola starting at $0$, max at $x = \pm 0.060$

$E_k = E_T - E_p$, inverted parabola
 Correct shape for $E_p$ and $E_k$ vs. $x$ 1 mark , correct energy levels and symmetry 1 mark

parts.content: Identify the amplitude, angular frequency, and phase constant.
parts.markscheme:
- $x_0 = 0.12\ \text{m}$, $\omega = 6.00\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$, $\phi = \frac{\pi}{4}$ 
- Correct units <marks> 1 mark </marks> and values <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the standard form
steps.content:
To solve this problem using **Pattern Matching**, we first need to recall the general equation for the displacement of an object in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM). This is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

The standard form of the displacement equation is:

$$x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $x_0$ is the **amplitude**
- $\omega$ is the **angular frequency**
- $\phi$ is the **phase constant**

steps.title: Match the given equation
steps.content:
Now, let's align the equation provided in the question directly underneath the standard form to see which parts correspond to each other:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= \phantom{0.1} x_0 \sin( \phantom{6} \omega t + \phi \,) \\ x(t) &= 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4}) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify Amplitude
steps.content:
By comparing the two equations, we can see that the coefficient in front of the sine function represents the amplitude ($x_0$).

$$x_0 = 0.12$$

Since the question specifies SI units, the unit for displacement and amplitude is **meters (m)**.

steps.title: Identify Angular Frequency
steps.content:
Next, look at the coefficient of the time variable ($t$) inside the parentheses. This corresponds to the angular frequency ($\omega$).

$$\omega = 6.00$$

The standard SI unit for angular frequency is **radians per second (rad s$^{-1}$)**.

steps.title: Identify Phase Constant
steps.content:
Finally, the constant value added to the time term inside the parentheses is the phase constant ($\phi$).

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

In SI units, phase constants are expressed in **radians (rad)**.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
To get full marks on an IB exam, you must provide both the correct values and the correct units.

- **Amplitude ($x_0$):** $0.12\ \text{m}$
- **Angular frequency ($\omega$):** $6.00\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- **Phase constant ($\phi$):** $\frac{\pi}{4}$ (or $\approx 0.785\ \text{rad}$)

gdc.instructions: Enter pi / 4 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type pi/4 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Convert pi/4 to decimal to check the phase constant value if needed.

steps.title: Identify general SHM form
steps.content:
To solve this using numerical analysis, we compare the given displacement equation to the general mathematical model for simple harmonic motion (SHM):

$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $A$ (or $x_0$) is the **amplitude**
- $\omega$ is the **angular frequency**
- $\phi$ is the **phase constant**

The question provides the specific equation:

$$x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

steps.title: Identify the Amplitude
steps.content:
The amplitude is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position. Numerically, this is the value multiplying the sine function. 

Looking at the equation, we can see that the maximum value the function can reach is $0.12$. Since the problem uses SI units, the unit for displacement is meters (m).

$$x_0 = 0.12\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Identify the Angular Frequency
steps.content:
The angular frequency, $\omega$, represents how quickly the object oscillates in terms of radians per second. It is the coefficient of the time variable $t$ inside the sine function.

From the given equation, we identify the value paired with $t$:

$$\omega = 6\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the Phase Constant
steps.content:
The phase constant, $\phi$, determines the displacement of the system at $t = 0$. In our SHM model, this is the constant term added to the angular frequency term.

By direct comparison, we find:

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{4}\ \text{rad}$$

steps.title: Verify with Graphing Tool
steps.content:
We can verify these values by graphing the function. 
- The peak of the graph (highest point) confirms the **amplitude**.
- The time for one full cycle ($T$) allows us to calculate $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$.
- The value at $t=0$ confirms the **phase shift**.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Enter the function f1(x) = 0.12 * sin(6x + π/4).
3. Ensure the angle setting is in Radians.
4. Use the 'Analyze Graph' -> 'Maximum' tool to find the peak Y-value.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 0.12 * sin(6X + π/4).
3. Ensure the calculator is in RADIAN mode ([MODE] -> RADIAN).
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Press [TRACE] and move to the peak to see the Y value (0.12).

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the function into the expression bar: y = 0.12 * sin(6x + π/4).
2. Click on any peak to verify the Y-coordinate is 0.12.
3. Click on the Y-intercept to see the displacement at t=0.

calculator.description: Graph the displacement function to visualize the amplitude and phase.

parts.content: Calculate the velocity of the mass at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $v = \omega x_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi) = 6.00 \cdot 0.12 \cdot \cos(6 \cdot 0.50 + \frac{\pi}{4}) = 0.72 \cdot \cos(3 + \frac{\pi}{4}) \approx -0.58\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks> 
- Correct final answer with unit <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Relate velocity to displacement
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the velocity $v(t)$ of an object is defined as the rate of change of its displacement $x(t)$ with respect to time. Using calculus, we express this as:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the displacement function
steps.content:
We are given the displacement function:
$$x(t) = 0.12 \sin\left(6t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

To find the velocity, we differentiate this with respect to $t$. Using the chain rule (where the derivative of $\sin(ut + \phi)$ is $\omega \cos(ut + \phi)$):

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt} \left[ 0.12 \sin\left(6t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \right] \\ v(t) &= 0.12 \times 6 \cos\left(6t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ v(t) &= 0.72 \cos\left(6t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute the time value
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $t = 0.50\text{ s}$ into our velocity expression to find the velocity at that specific moment:

$$v(0.50) = 0.72 \cos\left(6(0.50) + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
$$v(0.50) = 0.72 \cos\left(3 + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$ 

**Note:** When calculating this, ensure your calculator is in **radians** mode, as the angular frequency ($6$) and phase constant ($\frac{\pi}{4}$) are in SI units (rad s$^{-1}$ and rad).

highlights.text: Calculate the velocity of the mass at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Evaluating the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} v(0.50) &\approx 0.72 \times (-0.7998...) \\ v(0.50) &\approx -0.5758... \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (matching the precision of the given values like $0.12$ and $0.50$):

$$v = -0.58 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Radian mode.
2. Enter the expression 0.72 * cos(3 + π / 4) into the main calculation screen.
3. Execute the calculation.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure 'RADIAN' is highlighted. Press [ENTER].
2. Press [2nd] [MODE] to quit.
3. Type: 0.72 * cos(3 + π / 4)
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Radians (check the wrench icon settings).
2. Type: 0.72 * cos(3 + pi / 4)

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity using the derived expression in radians mode.

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the **velocity-displacement relationship**, we first need to identify the key parameters of the simple harmonic motion (SHM) from the given equation. 

The general form for displacement is:
$$x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Comparing this to the given equation:
$$x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

We can identify:
- Amplitude ($x_0$) = $0.12\text{ m}$
- Angular frequency ($\omega$) = $6\text{ rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate displacement at 0.50s
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the displacement $x$ at the specific time $t = 0.50\text{ s}$ by substituting the value into the equation. Ensure your calculator is in **radians mode**.

$$\begin{aligned} x(0.50) &= 0.12 \sin(6 \cdot 0.50 + \frac{\pi}{4}) \\ x(0.50) &= 0.12 \sin(3 + \frac{\pi}{4}) \\ x(0.50) &\approx -0.072\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to radians. Calculate 0.12 * sin(3 + π / 4).

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is RADIANS. Enter 0.12 * sin(3 + π / 4).

desmos.instructions: Set to Radians. Type 0.12 * sin(3 + pi / 4).

calculator.description: Calculate the displacement at t = 0.50 seconds.

steps.title: Apply velocity-displacement relationship
steps.content:
Now we use the relationship between velocity $v$, angular frequency $\omega$, amplitude $x_0$, and displacement $x$ found in **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**.

$$v = \pm \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$$

Substituting our known values:
$$|v| = 6 \sqrt{0.12^2 - (-0.072)^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate velocity magnitude
steps.content:
We calculate the magnitude of the velocity using the values found in previous steps.

$$\begin{aligned} |v| &= 6 \sqrt{0.0144 - 0.00518} \\ |v| &= 6 \sqrt{0.00922} \\ |v| &\approx 0.58\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of velocity using the displacement relationship.

steps.title: Determine the direction
steps.content:
The formula $v = \pm \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$ provides two possible directions. Since velocity is the rate of change of displacement, $v(t) \propto \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. 

We check the sign of the cosine term at $t = 0.50\text{ s}$:
$$\cos(3 + \frac{\pi}{4}) \approx -0.80$$

Since the cosine value is negative, the mass is moving in the negative $x$ direction. 

$$v \approx -0.58\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate cos(3 + π / 4).

calculator.description: Check the sign of the cosine term to determine direction.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The velocity of the mass at $t = 0.50\text{ s}$ is approximately **$-0.58\text{ m s}^{-1}$**. 

In the IB markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for the correct substitution into the velocity equation and 1 mark for the final numerical answer with the correct sign and units.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
In kinematics (Topic A.1), velocity $v$ is defined as the instantaneous rate of change of displacement $x$ with respect to time $t$. 

Mathematically, this is the derivative:
$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

To find the velocity at $t = 0.50\text{ s}$, we need to evaluate the derivative of the given displacement function at that specific time.

steps.title: Prepare your GDC
steps.content: Before performing any calculations involving trigonometric functions in Physics, it is crucial to check your calculator settings. Since the angular frequency $\omega$ and the phase constant $\phi$ are given in terms of $\pi$, your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) **must be in Radian mode**.

steps.title: Calculate numerical derivative
steps.content:
We can use the numerical differentiation feature of the GDC to find the slope of the displacement-time graph at $t = 0.50$. This gives us the velocity directly without needing to differentiate the formula by hand.

Using the function:
$$f(x) = 0.12 \sin(6x + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

We evaluate $\frac{d}{dx}f(x)$ at $x = 0.50$.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, press 'OPTN', then 'F4' (CALC), then 'F2' (d/dx). Enter the expression as: d/dx(0.12*sin(6*x + π/4), 0.50)

ti84.instructions: Press 'MATH', then select '8:nDeriv('. Enter the expression as: nDeriv(0.12*sin(6*X + π/4), X, 0.50)

desmos.instructions: Type the derivative operator followed by the function and the evaluation point.

calculator.description: Evaluate the derivative of the displacement function at t = 0.50.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator yields a result of approximately $-0.5758...\text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

Rounding to two decimal places as shown in the markscheme, we get:
$$v \approx -0.58\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

The negative sign indicates that the mass is moving in the negative $x$-direction at that instant.

parts.content: Determine the total mechanical energy of the system.
parts.markscheme:
- $E_T = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x_0^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 36 \cdot (0.12)^2 = 0.0648\ \text{J}$ 
- Correct final answer <marks> 1 mark </marks> with unit <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify parameters from the equation
steps.content:
To find the total energy, we first need to extract the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general form for simple harmonic motion is:
$$x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Comparing this to the provided equation:
$$x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

We can identify:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.12\text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 6\text{ rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Select the energy formula
steps.content:
The total mechanical energy ($E_T$) in a simple harmonic oscillator is constant and can be calculated using the mass, angular frequency, and amplitude. 

This formula is derived from the maximum kinetic energy, which occurs when the velocity is at its maximum ($v_{max} = \omega x_0$):
$$E_T = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x_0^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the energy equation:
- Mass $m = 0.25\text{ kg}$
- $\omega = 6\text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- $x_0 = 0.12\text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned} E_T &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \times 6^2 \times 0.12^2 \\ E_T &= 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 36 \times 0.0144 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the values into the calculation tool.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total mechanical energy.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The total mechanical energy is:
$$E_T = 0.0648\text{ J}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for the correct numerical calculation and **1 mark** for including the correct unit (Joules, J).

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To find the total mechanical energy, we first need to extract the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general form of the displacement equation for SHM is:
$$x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

By comparing this to the provided equation, we can see that:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.12\text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 6\text{ rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate maximum velocity
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the maximum velocity ($v_{\text{max}}$) occurs when the mass passes through the equilibrium position. It is calculated using the relationship:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 6 \times 0.12 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 0.72\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 6 multiplied by 0.12.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum velocity of the oscillating mass.

steps.title: Determine total mechanical energy
steps.content:
The total mechanical energy ($E_T$) of the system is equal to the maximum kinetic energy, which occurs at the equilibrium position where the velocity is $v_{\text{max}}$. 

Using the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

We substitute the mass and maximum velocity:
$$\begin{aligned} E_T &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \times (0.72)^2 \\ E_T &= 0.125 \times 0.5184 \\ E_T &= 0.0648\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.5 by the mass (0.25) and the square of the maximum velocity (0.72).

calculator.description: Calculate the total mechanical energy using the mass and maximum velocity.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
The total mechanical energy of the system is $0.0648\text{ J}$. 

In the markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for the correct numerical calculation and 1 mark for the correct unit (Joules).

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To find the energy, we first need to identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general form for simple harmonic motion (SHM) is:
$$x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Comparing this to the provided equation, we can see:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.12\text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 6\text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.25\text{ kg}$

steps.title: Calculate effective spring constant
steps.content:
In an oscillating system, the relationship between the mass, angular frequency, and the effective spring constant ($k$) is given by:

$$k = m\omega^2$$

Substituting our known values:
$$\begin{aligned} k &= 0.25 \cdot 6^2 \\ k &= 0.25 \cdot 36 \\ k &= 9\text{ N m}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, enter 0.25 * 6^2.

steps.title: Calculate total mechanical energy
steps.content:
The total mechanical energy in SHM is equal to the maximum elastic potential energy, which occurs at the maximum displacement (amplitude, $x_0$).

The formula for elastic potential energy is:
$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

Setting $x = x_0$ for total energy:
$$\begin{aligned} E_{total} &= \frac{1}{2}k(x_0)^2 \\ E_{total} &= 0.5 \cdot 9 \cdot (0.12)^2 \\ E_{total} &= 4.5 \cdot 0.0144 \\ E_{total} &= 0.0648\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, enter 0.5 * 9 * 0.12^2.

The total mechanical energy of the system is $0.0648\text{ J}$. 

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for the correct numerical calculation and one mark for providing the correct units (Joules).

parts.content: Calculate the potential energy of the system at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme:

- $E_p = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 36 \cdot (-0.0720)^2 \approx 0.0233\ \text{J}$
- Correct substitution <marks> 1 mark </marks> and final answer <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To find the potential energy using the Step-wise Displacement Method, we first need to identify the mass and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given simple harmonic motion (SHM) equation.

The general form for SHM displacement is:
$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Comparing this to the provided equation:
$$x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

We can see that:
- Amplitude $A = 0.12 \text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 6 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.25 \text{ kg}$

steps.title: Calculate displacement at time $t$
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the displacement $x$ at the specific time requested, which is $t = 0.50 \text{ s}$. Substitute this value into the displacement equation:

$$x(0.50) = 0.12 \sin\left(6 \cdot 0.50 + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
$$x(0.50) = 0.12 \sin\left(3 + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Using a calculator in **radians mode**, we find:
$$x \approx -0.0720 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle units to Radians in the Document Settings.
2. Type: 0.12 * sin(6 * 0.5 + π / 4)
3. Press [ENTER].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE], select 'RADIAN', press [ENTER].
2. Press [2nd] [QUIT].
3. Type: 0.12 * sin(6 * 0.50 + π / 4)
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Ensure 'Radians' is selected in the Graph Settings (wrench icon).
2. Type the expression: 0.12 * sin(6 * 0.5 + pi / 4)

calculator.description: Calculate the displacement x at t = 0.50 s. Ensure your calculator is in RADIAN mode.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy
steps.content:
The potential energy ($E_p$) in an SHM system is given by the formula:
$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2$$

This formula is derived from the elastic potential energy $E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$, where the effective spring constant $k$ is equal to $m\omega^2$. 

Now, substitute our known values:
- $m = 0.25 \text{ kg}$
- $\omega = 6 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- $x \approx -0.0720 \text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (6)^2 \cdot (-0.0720)^2 \\ E_p &= 0.125 \cdot 36 \cdot 0.005184 \\ E_p &\approx 0.0233 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final potential energy using the mass, angular frequency, and calculated displacement.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The potential energy of the system at $t = 0.50 \text{ s}$ is $0.0233 \text{ J}$.

According to the markscheme, you receive:
- **1 mark** for the correct substitution of $x$ into the energy formula.
- **1 mark** for the correct final answer (with units).

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
From the given displacement equation $x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$, we can identify the following parameters for the system:
- Mass ($m$) = $0.25\ \text{kg}$
- Amplitude ($A$) = $0.12\ \text{m}$
- Angular frequency ($\omega$) = $6\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- Phase constant ($\phi$) = $\frac{\pi}{4}$

This system follows **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. To find the potential energy at a specific time, we can combine the potential energy formula for SHM, $E_p = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2x^2$, with the time-dependent displacement equation.

steps.title: Define the Direct Energy Function
steps.content:
We combine the formulas to create a single expression for potential energy as a function of time, $E_p(t)$. By substituting $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ into the energy equation, we get:

$$E_p(t) = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 [A \sin(\omega t + \phi)]^2$$
$$E_p(t) = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t + \phi)$$

Now, we substitute the known values into this equation at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$:

$$E_p(0.50) = 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 6^2 \cdot 0.12^2 \cdot \sin^2(6 \cdot 0.50 + \frac{\pi}{4})$$

steps.title: Calculate potential energy
steps.content:
Using a calculator (set to **radians**), we evaluate the entire expression simultaneously to find the energy. 

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= 0.125 \cdot 36 \cdot 0.0144 \cdot \sin^2(3.785...) \\ E_p &\approx 0.023347... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the mass and time), we get $0.0233\ \text{J}$.

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to RADIAN mode. Enter the expression: 0.5 * 0.25 * 6^2 * 0.12^2 * (sin(6 * 0.5 + π / 4))^2

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in RADIAN mode. Enter the expression: 0.5 * 0.25 * 6^2 * 0.12^2 * (sin(6 * 0.5 + π / 4))^2

desmos.instructions: Ensure Desmos is in Radian mode. Enter the expression in a single line.

calculator.description: Calculate potential energy directly using the substitution formula.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The potential energy of the system at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$ is:

$$E_p \approx 0.0233\ \text{J}$$

In an IB exam, you would earn **1 mark** for the correct substitution of all values into the energy equation and **1 mark** for the final numerical answer with units.

steps.title: Identify SHM Parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using energy conservation, we first extract the key values from the given SHM equation $x(t) = 0.12 \sin(6t + \frac{\pi}{4})$:

- **Mass ($m$):** $0.25\ \text{kg}$
- **Amplitude ($A$):** $0.12\ \text{m}$
- **Angular frequency ($\omega$):** $6\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- **Time ($t$):** $0.50\ \text{s}$

steps.title: Calculate Total Energy
steps.content:
The total energy ($E_t$) in an oscillating system is constant and can be calculated using the amplitude. This concept is covered in **Topic C.1.3: Energy transformations in oscillations**.

$$E_t = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2$$

Substituting our values:
$$E_t = 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 6^2 \cdot 0.12^2 = 0.0648\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy of the system.

steps.title: Find Velocity at Time t
steps.content:
The velocity $v(t)$ is the derivative of the displacement $x(t)$. For $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the velocity is:

$$v(t) = A\omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

At $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$ (ensure your calculator is in **radians** mode):
$$v(0.50) = 0.12 \cdot 6 \cdot \cos(6 \cdot 0.50 + \frac{\pi}{4})$$
$$v(0.50) \approx -0.5759\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in radian mode. Enter the expression.

ti84.instructions: Press [MODE], select RADIANS. Then enter the expression.

desmos.instructions: Ensure Desmos is in Radian mode (default). Enter the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity at t = 0.50 s in radians.

steps.title: Calculate Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
Now we find the kinetic energy ($E_k$) at this specific moment using the formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$$E_k = 0.5 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (-0.5759)^2 \approx 0.0415\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy.

steps.title: Solve for Potential Energy
steps.content:
By the principle of **Conservation of Energy**, the total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy:

$$E_t = E_k + E_p \implies E_p = E_t - E_k$$

$$E_p = 0.0648 - 0.04145 \approx 0.0233\ \text{J}$$

This confirms the potential energy at $t = 0.50\ \text{s}$ is approximately $0.0233\ \text{J}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the subtraction.

steps.title: Identify the standard equation
steps.content:
In **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, we learn that the displacement $x$ of an oscillating mass can be described using a cosine or sine function. The standard form for an object starting at its maximum displacement is:

$$x = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

In this equation:
- $x_0$ (or $A$) represents the **amplitude** (maximum displacement).
- $\omega$ represents the angular frequency.

steps.title: Compare the equations
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the specific equation provided in the question and align it with our standard form:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Standard:} & \quad x = \mathbf{x_0} \cos(\omega t) \\ \text{Given:} & \quad x(t) = \mathbf{0.08} \cos(10t) \end{aligned}$$

By comparing the two, we can see that the coefficient in front of the cosine term directly represents the amplitude.

steps.title: Visualize with a graph
steps.content: To confirm this, you can graph the function. The amplitude is the height of the peaks from the center line ($x=0$). On a graphing calculator, you will see the graph oscillating between $+0.08$ and $-0.08$.

3. Set the view window so the y-axis shows -0.1 to 0.1.
4. Use the 'Max' tool to find the highest point on the curve.

3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=2, Ymin=-0.1, Ymax=0.1.
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 'maximum' to find the peak value.

desmos.instructions:

2. Observe the y-intercept or the peaks of the wave.
3. Click on a peak to see its coordinates; the y-value is the amplitude.
