parts.content:
At a certain instant, the displacement of the particle is $x=0.2 \text{ m}$. Calculate:

(i) the velocity of the particle at this displacement, and

(ii) the total energy of the system.
parts.markscheme:
(i) Velocity at $x=0.2 \text{ m}$:
- Use the velocity-displacement formula: $v = \pm \omega \sqrt{x_0^2-x^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values: $v = \pm 5 \sqrt{0.25^2-0.2^2} = \pm 5 \sqrt{0.0625-0.04}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final velocity: $v = \pm 5 \sqrt{0.0225} = \pm 5 \times 0.15 = \pm 0.75 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

(ii) Total energy:
- Use the total energy formula: $E_T=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x_0^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values: $E_T=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 5^2 \times 0.25^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate total energy: $E_T=0.1 \times 25 \times 0.0625=0.156 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Explain how the total energy of the system is distributed between kinetic energy and potential energy at $x=0.2 \text{ m}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate potential energy ($E_P$) at $x=0.2 \text{ m}$ using $E_P=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$: $E_P=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 5^2 \times 0.2^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Obtain potential energy value: $E_P=0.1 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate kinetic energy ($E_K$) as the remainder: $E_K=E_T-E_P=0.156-0.1=0.056 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain distribution: At $x=0.2 \text{ m}$, most of the energy ($0.1 \text{ J}$) is in potential form, while the remainder ($0.056 \text{ J}$) is kinetic energy. <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the Total Energy Subtraction approach, we first need to identify the mass $m$, the angular frequency $\omega$, and the amplitude $x_0$ from the given displacement equation.

The general form for SHM is $x = x_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. Comparing this to our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= 0.25 \text{ m} \\ \omega &= 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1} \\ m &= 0.2 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

This information is essential for calculating the energy components.

steps.title: Calculate potential energy
steps.content:
First, we calculate the potential energy ($E_P$) at the specific displacement $x = 0.2 \text{ m}$. In SHM, potential energy is given by:

$$E_P = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$$

Substituting our known values:

$$E_P = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 5^2 \times 0.2^2$$

$$E_P = 0.1 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.2 * 5^2 * 0.2^2 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the potential energy value.

steps.title: Calculate total energy
steps.content:
Next, we find the total energy ($E_T$) of the system. The total energy remains constant and is equal to the potential energy at the maximum displacement (amplitude $x_0 = 0.25 \text{ m}$):

$$E_T = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x_0^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_T = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 5^2 \times 0.25^2$$

$$E_T = 0.15625 \approx 0.156 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.2 * 5^2 * 0.25^2 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy using the amplitude.

steps.title: Determine kinetic energy
steps.content:
The kinetic energy ($E_K$) is the remaining part of the total energy after accounting for potential energy. We use the subtraction method:

$$E_K = E_T - E_P$$

$$E_K = 0.156 - 0.1 = 0.056 \text{ J}$$

This method is a very efficient way to find kinetic energy when the displacement is known, as it avoids needing to calculate the velocity at that specific point.

steps.title: Explain energy distribution
steps.content:
Finally, we describe how the energy is distributed at $x = 0.2 \text{ m}$. 

At this position, the total energy is $0.156 \text{ J}$. Most of this energy, **$0.1 \text{ J}$**, is stored as **potential energy**, while the smaller remainder, **$0.056 \text{ J}$**, exists as **kinetic energy**. This indicates the particle is moving toward the amplitude point where kinetic energy will eventually become zero.

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the velocity-based approach, we first extract the amplitude ($x_0$) and angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation:

$$x = 0.25 \cos \left(5t - \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

From the standard form $x = x_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, we identify:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.2 \text{ kg}$

steps.title: Calculate instantaneous velocity
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the velocity $v$ of the particle at the specific displacement $x = 0.2 \text{ m}$ using the SHM velocity-displacement relationship:

$$v = \pm \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= 5 \times \sqrt{0.25^2 - 0.2^2} \\ v &= 5 \times \sqrt{0.0625 - 0.04} \\ v &= 5 \times \sqrt{0.0225} \\ v &= 5 \times 0.15 \\ v &= 0.75 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 5 * sqrt(0.25^2 - 0.2^2) and execute.

desmos.instructions: Type 5 * sqrt(0.25^2 - 0.2^2) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity at x = 0.2 m.

steps.title: Determine kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the kinetic energy ($E_k$) at this position using the standard kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times (0.75)^2 \\ E_k &= 0.1 \times 0.5625 \\ E_k &\approx 0.056 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.2 * 0.75^2 and execute.

calculator.description: Calculate kinetic energy.

steps.title: Determine potential energy
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the potential energy ($E_p$) at a displacement $x$ is given by:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2x^2$$

Substituting the known values at $x = 0.2 \text{ m}$:
$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 5^2 \times 0.2^2 \\ E_p &= 0.1 \times 25 \times 0.04 \\ E_p &= 0.1 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.2 * 5^2 * 0.2^2 and execute.

calculator.description: Calculate potential energy.

steps.title: Explain energy distribution
steps.content:
To explain the distribution, we compare the two values. At the displacement of $0.2 \text{ m}$:

- **Potential Energy ($E_p$):** $0.1 \text{ J}$
- **Kinetic Energy ($E_k$):** $0.056 \text{ J}$

Most of the system's total energy ($E_T = 0.156 \text{ J}$) is stored as potential energy ($0.1 \text{ J}$), while the remaining $0.056 \text{ J}$ exists as kinetic energy. This happens because $0.2 \text{ m}$ is close to the maximum amplitude ($0.25 \text{ m}$), where the particle slows down and energy is shifted into the potential form.

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To understand how energy is distributed, we first need to identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general form is $x = x_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. Comparing this to our equation:

$$x=0.25 \cos \left(5 t-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

We can see that:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.2 \text{ kg}$

steps.title: Calculate total system energy
steps.content:
The total energy ($E_T$) in a simple harmonic oscillator is constant and depends on the amplitude. It is given by the formula:

$$E_T = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x_0^2$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_T &= \frac{1}{2} (0.2) (5)^2 (0.25)^2 \\ E_T &= 0.1 \times 25 \times 0.0625 \\ E_T &= 0.15625 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three decimal places as seen in the markscheme, $E_T \approx 0.156 \text{ J}$.

gdc.instructions: Input 0.5 * 0.2 * 5^2 * 0.25^2 and evaluate.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy of the system.

steps.title: Determine potential energy fraction
steps.content:
Using the **Fractional Energy Approach**, we know that potential energy ($E_P$) is proportional to the square of the displacement ($x^2$). Therefore, the fraction of total energy that is potential is:

$$\text{Fraction}_P = \frac{x^2}{x_0^2}$$

At the specific displacement $x = 0.2 \text{ m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Fraction}_P &= \frac{(0.2)^2}{(0.25)^2} \\ \text{Fraction}_P &= \frac{0.04}{0.0625} \\ \text{Fraction}_P &= 0.64 \end{aligned}$$

This means that $64\%$ of the total energy is stored as potential energy at this point.

gdc.instructions: Input 0.2^2 / 0.25^2 and evaluate.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the squares to find the energy fraction.

steps.title: Calculate energy distribution
steps.content:
Now we can find the specific values for potential and kinetic energy ($E_K$):

1. **Potential Energy ($E_P$):**
$$E_P = \text{Fraction}_P \times E_T = 0.64 \times 0.15625 = 0.1 \text{ J}$$

2. **Kinetic Energy ($E_K$):**
According to the conservation of energy (Topic A.3.1), the remaining fraction $(1 - 0.64 = 0.36)$ must be kinetic energy:
$$E_K = E_T - E_P = 0.15625 - 0.1 = 0.05625 \text{ J}$$

Rounding to significant figures, we get $E_K \approx 0.056 \text{ J}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.64 * 0.15625 for Ep and 0.15625 - 0.1 for Ek.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the difference between total and potential energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy values.

steps.title: Final distribution summary
steps.content:
At $x=0.2 \text{ m}$, the energy is distributed such that:
- **Potential Energy ($E_P$)** is $0.1 \text{ J}$.
- **Kinetic Energy ($E_K$)** is $0.056 \text{ J}$.

Because the particle is relatively far from the equilibrium position (at $80\%$ of its maximum displacement), a larger portion of the energy ($64\%$) is potential, leaving only the remainder as kinetic energy.

parts.markscheme:

1 mark total

steps.title: Recall SHM requirements
steps.content:
To understand the motion of a pendulum, we first look at the defining feature of **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. For any system to undergo SHM, the restoring force (and thus the acceleration) must be directly proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position and directed towards it.

This topic is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. If you need a refresher on the basics of oscillation, that’s the place to look!

steps.title: Analyze the restoring force
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum, the restoring force $F$ that pulls the mass back toward the center is given by:

$$F = -mg \sin\theta$$

In SHM, we need the force to be proportional to the displacement $x$ (or the angle $\theta$). However, the force here depends on $\sin\theta$, which is not a linear relationship.

steps.title: Apply small angle condition
steps.content:
To make the motion "simple harmonic," we use the **small angle approximation**. When the angle $\theta$ is small (measured in radians), then $\sin\theta \approx \theta$. 

Under this condition, the restoring force becomes approximately proportional to the displacement:

$$F \approx -mg\theta$$

In the IB curriculum, this "small" angle is generally defined as being less than about $10^\circ$.

steps.title: State the final condition
steps.content:
The condition required for the pendulum to undergo SHM is that the angular displacement must be small.

**Final Answer:** The angular displacement $\theta$ must be small (typically $\theta < 10^\circ$).

highlights.text: State that for small angular displacements (\theta < 10^\circ) the motion approximates simple harmonic motion

parts.content: Outline why the restoring force on the pendulum bob is proportional to the displacement for small angles $\theta$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the restoring force component along the arc: $F = -mg\sin\theta$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Apply small angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians) and $s = L\theta$ to show $F \propto -s$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

methods.label: Force Resolution and Small Angle Approximation

steps.title: Identify the restoring force
steps.content:
When the pendulum bob is displaced, gravity ($mg$) acts vertically downwards. We can resolve this weight into two components: one acting along the string (balanced by tension) and one acting tangentially to the path (the restoring force). 

The restoring force $F$ acts along the arc towards the equilibrium position and is given by:

$$F = -mg \sin\theta$$

The negative sign indicates that the force is always directed back toward the center.

steps.title: Apply small angle approximation
steps.content:
In IB Physics, specifically in **Topic C.1 (Simple Harmonic Motion)**, we learn that for small angles (typically $\theta < 10^\circ$ or $0.17$ rad), the value of the sine of the angle is approximately equal to the angle itself in radians.

Applying the small angle approximation:

$$\sin\theta \approx \theta$$

Substituting this into our force equation, we get:

$$F \approx -mg\theta$$

steps.title: Relate angle to displacement
steps.content:
The displacement $s$ of the bob is the arc length it has traveled from the equilibrium position. From the geometry of a circle, the arc length $s$ is related to the radius $L$ and the angle $\theta$ (in radians) by the formula:

$$s = L\theta \implies \theta = \frac{s}{L}$$

Now, we substitute this expression for $\theta$ back into our force equation:

$$F = -mg\left(\frac{s}{L}\right)$$

$$F = -\left(\frac{mg}{L}\right)s$$

steps.title: Show proportionality
steps.content:
Since the mass $m$, the acceleration due to gravity $g$, and the length of the pendulum $L$ are all constants for a given setup, the term $\left(\frac{mg}{L}\right)$ is a constant.

Therefore, we have shown that:

$$F \propto -s$$

This demonstrates that the restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement and acts in the opposite direction, which is the defining condition for Simple Harmonic Motion.

steps.title: Identify the restoring force
steps.content:
In a pendulum, the **restoring force** ($F$) is the component of the weight that acts to pull the bob back toward its equilibrium position. 

At any angle $\theta$, the weight ($mg$) acts vertically downwards. We can decompose this weight into two components: one acting along the string and one acting perpendicular to it (the restoring force).

steps.title: Use similar triangles
steps.content:
For a **small angle** $\theta$, the triangle formed by the forces (the weight and the restoring force) is geometrically similar to the triangle formed by the pendulum's displacement (the length $L$ and horizontal displacement $x$).

By comparing the ratios of corresponding sides in these similar triangles, we get:

$$\frac{F}{mg} \approx \frac{x}{L}$$

Where:
- $F$ is the restoring force.
- $mg$ is the weight (acting as the hypotenuse in the force triangle for very small angles).
- $x$ is the horizontal displacement.
- $L$ is the length of the pendulum.

steps.title: Establish proportionality
steps.content:
We can rearrange the ratio from the previous step to solve for the restoring force $F$:

$$F \approx \left( \frac{mg}{L} \right) x$$

Since the mass $m$, the gravitational field strength $g$, and the length $L$ are all constant for a given pendulum, the term in the brackets is a constant. 

This shows that $F$ is directly proportional to the displacement $x$:

$$F \propto x$$

gdc.instructions: In Radian mode, evaluate the sine of a small value like 0.05 and observe how close it is to the input value.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Radian mode. Calculate sin(0.1) and compare it to 0.1.

desmos.instructions: Type sin(0.1) to see that it is approximately 0.0998, which is nearly 0.1.

calculator.description: You can verify the small angle approximation (sin θ ≈ θ) for yourself. At small angles, the ratio x/L is essentially equal to the angle θ in radians.

steps.title: Link to Simple Harmonic Motion
steps.content:
In IB Physics, showing that the restoring force is proportional to the displacement ($F \propto -x$) is the fundamental requirement for an object to undergo **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. 

This relationship only holds true when the angle is small (typically $\theta < 10^\circ$), as the geometric similarity between the arc length and horizontal displacement $x$ begins to fail at larger angles.

parts.content: Calculate the period of oscillation of the pendulum if $L = 0.90\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the period formula: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.90}{9.81}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer: $T = 1.90\ \text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the period formula
steps.content:
To calculate the period of oscillation for a simple pendulum, we use the specific formula for its time period. This concept is explored in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, which describes how the period depends on the length of the pendulum and the strength of gravity.

$$T = 2	ext{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the equation. We are given the length of the pendulum and we use the standard value for the acceleration due to gravity, $g \approx 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$.

$$\begin{aligned} L &= 0.90\text{ m} \\ g &= 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting these into our formula gives:

$$T = 2\text{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.90}{9.81}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final period
steps.content:
Finally, we perform the calculation. Following the order of operations, first divide the length by gravity, take the square root, and then multiply by $2\text{\pi}$.

$$T \approx 1.90\text{ s}$$

This result is rounded to three significant figures, matching the precision of the data provided in the question.

gdc.instructions: Use the square root and π keys to evaluate the expression.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] to get π. Enter the expression as shown.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the period of the pendulum using the direct formula.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To calculate the period of oscillation, we first need to identify the known variables from the question. We are given the length of the pendulum, and we know the acceleration due to gravity.

- Length of the pendulum, $L = 0.90\ \text{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g \approx 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (standard value for IB questions)

This problem relates to **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, which covers the oscillatory behavior of systems like pendulums.

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum undergoing small oscillations, the angular frequency $\omega$ is related to the length and gravity by the following relationship:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.90}}$$

$$\omega \approx 3.302\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root of (9.81 divided by 0.90).

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(9.81/0.90) into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency ω.

steps.title: Determine the period
steps.content:
Now that we have the angular frequency, we can find the period $T$. The relationship between period and angular frequency is:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Substituting our calculated $\omega$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{3.302}$$

$$T \approx 1.90\ \text{s}$$

This two-step process confirms how the length directly influences the time it takes for one full swing.

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 times pi by the previously calculated value of omega.

calculator.description: Calculate the period T using the angular frequency.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The period of oscillation for the pendulum is $1.90\ \text{s}$. 

In an IB exam, you would earn marks as follows:
- **M1**: Correct substitution of values into a recognized period formula.
- **A1**: Correct final answer with appropriate units ($s$) and significant figures (usually 2 or 3 based on the given data).

questions.specification:
A particle undergoing simple harmonic motion (SHM) is described by the displacement equation:

$$
x=0.15 \sin \left(10 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)
$$

where $x$ is in meters and $t$ is in seconds. The mass of the particle is 0.5 kg.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is entirely about analyzing an SHM displacement equation x = A sin(ωt + φ): identifying amplitude/angular frequency/phase and differentiating to obtain velocity, then evaluating v at a specific time.

parts.content: Identify the amplitude, angular frequency, and phase angle of the motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Amplitude ($x_0$): $0.15 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Angular frequency ($\omega$): $10 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Phase angle ($\phi$): $\frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the standard SHM form
steps.content:
To solve this, we compare the given displacement equation to the standard general form of a simple harmonic motion (SHM) equation. In **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, we learn that the displacement $x$ of an oscillating particle can be represented as:

$$x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $x_0$ is the **amplitude**.
- $\omega$ is the **angular frequency**.
- $\phi$ is the **phase angle** (or phase constant).

steps.title: Compare and identify amplitude
steps.content:
Look at the coefficient in front of the sine function in the provided equation:

$$x = 0.15 \sin \left(10 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

By direct comparison with $x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, we can see that the amplitude $x_0$ is the value multiplying the sine term.

$$x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

This earns you the first mark.

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
Next, we look at the term multiplying the time $t$ inside the parentheses. This corresponds to the angular frequency $\omega$.

Comparing $10t$ from the question to $\omega t$ from the general form:

$$\omega = 10 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

This is your second mark.

steps.title: Identify the phase angle
steps.content:
Finally, identify the constant value added to the time term inside the sine function. This is the phase angle $\phi$, which tells us the position of the particle at $t = 0$.

Comparing $+\frac{\pi}{4}$ to $+\phi$:

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad}$$

This completes the 3 marks for this part.

steps.title: Identify the function
steps.content:
To solve this using graphical analysis, we first identify the displacement function given in the question. This describes the position of the particle over time $t$:

$$x = 0.15 \sin\left(10t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

We will use a graphing calculator to visualize this motion by plotting $y = 0.15 \sin(10x + \frac{\pi}{4})$, where $y$ represents displacement and $x$ represents time.

steps.title: Plot the graph
steps.content:
Using your graphing tool, plot the function and adjust the window to see a few full oscillations. 

Suggested window settings:
- $x$-axis (time): $0$ to $2$
- $y$-axis (displacement): $-0.2$ to $0.2$

gdc.instructions: In the graph menu, enter the function 0.15*sin(10x + π/4). Adjust the view window for time (0 to 2) and displacement (-0.2 to 0.2).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=], enter 0.15*sin(10X + π/4). Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=2, Ymin=-0.2, Ymax=0.2. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the expression bar. Use the wrench icon to set the x-axis from 0 to 2 and y-axis from -0.2 to 0.2.

calculator.description: Plot the SHM displacement function.

steps.title: Determine the Amplitude
steps.content:
The amplitude ($x_0$) is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium ($x=0$). On your graph, use the 'Maximum' tool to find the highest point on the curve.

The calculator shows the $y$-coordinate of the peak is $0.15$.

$$x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the G-Solv or Analysis tool to find the 'Max' value.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC), select '4:maximum'. Set left and right bounds around a peak, then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click on any peak of the graph to display its coordinates; the y-value is the amplitude.

calculator.description: Find the maximum y-value of the function.

steps.title: Calculate Angular Frequency
steps.content:
To find the angular frequency ($\omega$), we first determine the period ($T$) by measuring the time between two consecutive peaks on the graph. 

The distance between peaks is approximately $0.628$ seconds. We then use the relationship from the IB syllabus:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{0.628...} = 10 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2π / T using the measured period.

calculator.description: Calculate angular frequency from the period.

steps.title: Identify the Phase Angle
steps.content:
The phase angle ($\phi$) represents the constant offset inside the sine function. In the general SHM equation $x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, $\phi$ is the value added to the time-dependent term.

From the given equation, we identify:

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad}$$

highlights.text: Identify the amplitude, angular frequency, and phase angle

parts.content: Derive an expression for the velocity $v$ of the particle as a function of time, starting from the given displacement equation.
parts.markscheme:
- State that velocity is the time derivative of displacement: $v=\frac{d x}{d t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Differentiate to find the general expression: $v=\omega x_0 \cos (\omega t+\phi)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute correct values $\omega=10$ and $x_0=0.15$: $v=10 \times 0.15 \cos \left(10 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the simplified final answer: $v=1.5 \cos \left(10 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the velocity of the particle at $t=0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $t=0$ into the velocity equation: $v=1.5 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the exact value: $v=1.5 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the final answer: $v=1.06 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Differentiate displacement for velocity
steps.content:
In kinematics, velocity $v$ is defined as the rate of change of displacement $x$ with respect to time $t$. We find the velocity function by differentiating the given displacement equation:

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{dx}{dt} \\ v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt} \left[ 0.15 \sin \left( 10t + \frac{\pi}{4} \right) \right] \end{aligned}$$

Using the chain rule, where the derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u) \cdot \frac{du}{dt}$:

$$v(t) = 0.15 \times 10 \cos \left( 10t + \frac{\pi}{4} \right)$$
$$v(t) = 1.5 \cos \left( 10t + \frac{\pi}{4} \right)$$ 

This uses concepts from **Topic A.1: Kinematics** regarding the relationship between displacement and velocity.

steps.title: Substitute time t=0
steps.content:
To find the velocity at the specific instant $t=0$, we substitute zero into our velocity function. This is the first step toward earning marks in the markscheme.

$$v(0) = 1.5 \cos \left( 10(0) + \frac{\pi}{4} \right)$$
$$v(0) = 1.5 \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{4} \right)$$

This substitution earns the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Calculate the final velocity
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the expression. Recall that $\cos \left( \frac{\pi}{4} \right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707$.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 1.5 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \\ v &\approx 1.5 \times 0.7071 \\ v &\approx 1.06066... \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$v = 1.06 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This final value earns the accuracy marks (**A1**, **A1**).

gdc.instructions: Set the calculator to RADIAN mode. Enter the expression '1.5 * cos(π / 4)' into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in RADIAN mode. Press [MODE], select 'RADIAN', then press [ENTER]. Go to the main screen and type '1.5 * cos(π / 4)' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Make sure the angle units are set to Radians in the settings. Type the expression into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity at t=0 using the expression 1.5 * cos(pi/4).

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
From the given displacement equation, we can identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) by comparing it to the general form $$x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$.

Comparing this to the given equation:
- Amplitude $$x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$
- Angular frequency $$\omega = 10 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate displacement at $t=0$
steps.content:
We substitute the time into the displacement equation to find the position of the particle at the start of the motion.

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 0.15 \sin \left(10(0) + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ x &= 0.15 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Using the exact value $$\sin(\frac{\pi}{4}) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$$:
$$x = 0.15 \times 0.7071 = 0.106066... \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 0.15 * sin(π/4) using radians.

calculator.description: Calculate the displacement x at t=0.

steps.title: Apply velocity-displacement relation
steps.content:
The relationship between velocity ($v$), angular frequency ($\omega$), amplitude ($x_0$), and displacement ($x$) in simple harmonic motion is given in the data booklet:
$$v = \pm \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$$

Substituting our values:
$$v = \pm 10 \sqrt{0.15^2 - 0.106066^2}$$

Since the particle is starting from a phase of $\frac{\pi}{4}$ with a sine function, it is moving in the positive direction at $t=0$. Thus, we use the positive sign.

steps.title: Calculate the final velocity
steps.content:
Now we perform the final numerical calculation using the values from previous steps:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 10 \sqrt{0.0225 - 0.01125} \\ v &= 10 \sqrt{0.01125} \\ v &\approx 1.06066... \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:
$$v = 1.06 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final velocity v.

steps.title: Define velocity from displacement
steps.content:
In kinematics, velocity $v$ is the instantaneous rate of change of displacement $x$ with respect to time $t$. Mathematically, this is the derivative:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

To find the velocity at a specific moment using your Graphing Calculator (GDC), you can use the numerical derivative function at the given point $t = 0$.

Substituting $t=0$ into the derivative function will give us the velocity directly.

parts.content: Calculate the new period of oscillation for the platform with the package on it.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the total mass: $m_{\text{total}} = 15.0 + 2.00 = 17.0\ \text{kg}$
- Substitute into the period formula: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{17.0}{900}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final answer: $T = 0.864\ \text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the total mass
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the total mass that is oscillating. Since the package is placed on the platform, the total mass ($m$) is the sum of the platform's mass and the package's mass.

$$m = m_{\text{platform}} + m_{\text{package}}$$

$$m = 15.0 + 2.00 = 17.0\ \text{kg}$$

steps.title: Identify the period formula
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system undergoing simple harmonic motion (SHM), the period of oscillation ($T$) depends on the mass ($m$) and the spring constant ($k$). This relationship is a fundamental equation in **Topic C.1 Simple Harmonic Motion**:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$ 

We are given the spring constant $k = 900\ \text{N m}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, substitute the total mass ($m = 17.0\ \text{kg}$) and the spring constant ($k = 900\ \text{N m}^{-1}$) into the equation:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{17.0}{900}}$$ 

Use your calculator to evaluate this expression.

gdc.instructions: Type 2 * pi * sqrt(17/900) and press EXE

calculator.description: Calculate the period using the square root function.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives:

$$T \approx 0.8635...\ \text{s}$$ 

Rounding to 3 significant figures (consistent with the data provided):

$$T = 0.864\ \text{s}$$

steps.title: Determine the total mass
steps.content:
The oscillating system consists of both the platform and the package. First, we calculate the total mass $m$ by adding the mass of the platform and the package.

$$m = 15.0 + 2.00 = 17.0\ \text{kg}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total mass

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
Using the angular frequency approach for a spring-mass system, we determine $\omega$ using the spring constant $k$ and total mass $m$.

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{900}{17.0}}$$

$$\omega \approx 7.276\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(900 / 17.0)

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x^2] (to get square root), then enter 900 / 17.0

calculator.description: Calculate angular frequency

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
Now we calculate the period $T$ using the relationship between period and angular frequency.

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Substituting our calculated value for $\omega$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{7.276}$$

$$T \approx 0.8635\ \text{s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi / Ans

calculator.description: Calculate the period

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question provides data to 3 significant figures, so we round our final answer accordingly.

$$T = 0.864\ \text{s}$$

parts.content: Determine the maximum speed of the system if its amplitude is $0.040\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate angular frequency: $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{0.864} \approx 7.27\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- Substitute into the maximum speed formula: $v_{\text{max}} = \omega A = 7.27 \times 0.040$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final answer: $v_{\text{max}} = 0.291\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the total mass
steps.content:
To find the maximum speed of the oscillating system, we first need to determine the total mass $m$ that is moving. Since the package is placed on the platform and they move together, we add their masses:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{total} &= m_{platform} + m_{package} \\ m_{total} &= 15.0\text{ kg} + 2.00\text{ kg} \\ m_{total} &= 17.0\text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: platform has a mass of 15.0 kg

steps.title: Find the angular frequency
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) for a mass-spring system, the angular frequency $\omega$ is related to the spring constant $k$ and the mass $m$ by the formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \sqrt{\frac{900}{17.0}} \\ \omega &\approx 7.276\text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function and divide 900 by 17.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency omega.

highlights.text: spring with a spring constant k = 900 N m^{-1}

steps.title: Calculate maximum speed
steps.content:
The maximum speed $v_{\text{max}}$ in SHM occurs at the equilibrium position and is given by the formula:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega A$$

Using the amplitude $A = 0.040\text{ m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 7.276 \times 0.040 \\ v_{\text{max}} &\approx 0.291\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the angular frequency result by 0.04.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result (ANS) by 0.040 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type '7.276 * 0.04' or use the previous result variable.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum speed by multiplying omega by the amplitude.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The maximum speed of the platform-package system is $0.291\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** (M1) for correctly substituting your calculated $\omega$ and the given amplitude into the formula, and **1 mark** (A1) for providing the final answer with correct units.

steps.title: Calculate total system mass
steps.content:
Since the package of mass $2.00\text{ kg}$ is placed on the $15.0\text{ kg}$ platform, they move together as a single oscillating system. We must use the total mass $M$ for our calculations.

$$\begin{aligned} M &= m_{p} + m_{pkg} \\ M &= 15.0\text{ kg} + 2.00\text{ kg} \\ M &= 17.0\text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State conservation of energy
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion (SHM), energy continuously transforms between kinetic energy and potential energy. The total mechanical energy is conserved. 

The maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$) occurs at the equilibrium position where all energy is kinetic ($E_{k,\text{max}}$). The maximum displacement (amplitude $A$) occurs when all energy is elastic potential energy ($E_{p,\text{max}}$). Therefore:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = E_{p,\text{max}}$$

steps.title: Apply energy formulas
steps.content:
We use the formulas for kinetic energy and elastic potential energy from **Topic A.3 (Work, Energy, and Power)**:

$$\frac{1}{2}Mv_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{1}{2}kA^2$$

Where:
- $M = 17.0\text{ kg}$
- $k = 900\text{ N m}^{-1}$
- $A = 0.040\text{ m}$

steps.title: Solve for maximum speed
steps.content:
Rearrange the energy equation to solve for $v_{\text{max}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} Mv_{\text{max}}^2 &= kA^2 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= \sqrt{\frac{k A^2}{M}} \\ v_{\text{max}} &= A \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the values:

$$v_{\text{max}} = 0.040 \times \sqrt{\frac{900}{17.0}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.040 * sqrt(900 / 17) and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.040 * sqrt(900 / 17) in a new line.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum speed using the energy conservation formula.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
After calculating, we find:

$$v_{\text{max}} \approx 0.291\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This matches the result obtained via the angular frequency method, confirming our energy conservation approach is correct.

2 marks total

parts.content: Explain what would happen to the system’s motion if the platform were driven at a frequency equal to its natural frequency.
parts.markscheme:
- State that at resonance, the system absorbs energy most efficiently, leading to large amplitude oscillations <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that this could cause damage or failure due to excessive vibrations <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the phenomenon
steps.content:
The question states that the platform is being driven at a frequency equal to its natural frequency. According to **Resonance Theory**, this specific condition is defined as **resonance**.

This concept is covered in **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

highlights.text: driven at a frequency equal to its natural frequency

steps.title: Explain amplitude and energy
steps.content:
At resonance, the periodic driving force is synchronized with the natural motion of the system. This allows for **maximum energy transfer** from the driver to the oscillating system.

As the system absorbs this energy, the **amplitude** of the oscillations increases significantly, reaching its maximum possible value (limited typically by damping).

steps.title: State the consequences
steps.content:
The resulting large amplitude oscillations create excessive vibrations. This can lead to structural failure, damage to the system, or the package losing contact with the platform (if the acceleration exceeds $g$).

**Answer Summary:**
The system enters **resonance**, absorbing energy most efficiently and causing **large amplitude oscillations** which may lead to **damage or failure**.

parts.content: Suggest one real-world application where understanding SHM resonance is important.
parts.markscheme:
- State a valid application, e.g., engineering of buildings and bridges to avoid structural resonance during earthquakes <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Recall the concept of resonance
steps.content:
In **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**, you learned that resonance occurs when an external driving force acts on a system at a frequency equal to the system's **natural frequency**. 

At resonance, the transfer of energy is maximum, causing the system to oscillate with a very large amplitude. In the context of **Structural Safety**, these large amplitudes can be dangerous because they produce immense stress on materials, potentially causing them to fail or collapse.

steps.title: Identify a structural safety hazard
steps.content:
Consider large structures like bridges or skyscrapers. These structures have their own natural frequencies of vibration. 

External forces such as **earthquakes** or strong **winds** act as driving forces. If the frequency of the earthquake shockwaves or wind gusts matches the natural frequency of the building or bridge, resonance will occur, leading to dangerous swaying or even catastrophic collapse.

steps.title: State the real-world application
steps.content:
Therefore, a critical real-world application is in **civil engineering**. Engineers must design buildings and bridges to ensure their natural frequencies do **not** match the expected frequencies of local earthquakes or wind patterns.

A valid answer would be:

**Designing buildings and bridges to withstand resonance caused by earthquakes or wind.**

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
The question asks us to suggest a real-world application where understanding **resonance** is important. 

In the context of **Technological Utility**, we are looking for a system designed to explicitly *utilize* resonance to function correctly, rather than just avoiding it.

highlights.text: Suggest one real-world application where understanding SHM resonance is important.

steps.title: Define resonance utility
steps.content:
Recall from **Topic C.4** that resonance occurs when a driving frequency matches the natural frequency of a system. 

At resonance, the transfer of energy to the system is maximized, leading to large amplitude oscillations. Many technologies rely on this maximum energy transfer.

steps.title: Select a specific application
steps.content:
A classic example of utilizing resonance is the **microwave oven**.

Inside the oven, an electronic component (magnetron) produces microwave radiation. The frequency of these waves is chosen to match the natural resonant frequency of water molecules found in food.

steps.title: Explain the mechanism
steps.content:
Because the driving frequency (microwaves) matches the natural frequency of the water molecules, **resonance** occurs.

This causes the water molecules to oscillate with maximum amplitude, effectively absorbing energy from the waves and converting it into thermal energy to heat the food.

steps.title: Alternative applications
steps.content:
Other valid examples where resonance is utilized include:
*   **Radio tuning circuits:** Adjusting a circuit's natural frequency to resonate with a specific radio station's broadcast frequency.
*   **Musical instruments:** Using the resonance of strings or air columns to amplify sound.

Note: The markscheme also accepts examples where resonance is dangerous and must be *avoided*, such as in the design of **bridges** or **buildings** to withstand earthquakes or wind.

parts.content: Determine the amplitude, angular frequency, and phase constant of the motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Amplitude $A = 0.15\ \text{m}$ and angular frequency $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
 - Phase constant is $0$ (since $\cos$ function starts at max displacement) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Setup the comparison
steps.content:
To find the constants of motion, we will use the **Standard Form Comparison** method. We place the given equation for displacement directly next to the general equation for simple harmonic motion (SHM) defined in **Topic C.1**.

Given equation:
$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$

General standard form:
$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

steps.title: Identify the amplitude
steps.content:
By comparing the coefficients in front of the cosine function, we can identify the amplitude $A$.

$$A = 0.15$$

The unit of displacement is given as meters, so the amplitude is **$0.15\ \text{m}$**.

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
Next, we look at the term multiplying the time variable $t$ inside the cosine function. This corresponds to the angular frequency $\omega$.

$$\omega = 5$$

The standard unit for angular frequency is radians per second, so $\omega = \mathbf{5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}}$.

steps.title: Identify the phase constant
steps.content:
Finally, we look for any constant term added to $\omega t$ inside the cosine function to find the phase constant $\phi$.

In the given equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$, the term inside the brackets is just $5t$. This is equivalent to:

$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t + 0)$$

Comparing this to $\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, we see that the phase constant is **$0$**.

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
We have successfully identified all three parameters by direct comparison:

*   **Amplitude:** $A = 0.15\ \text{m}$
*   **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
*   **Phase Constant:** $\phi = 0$

steps.title: Analyze the displacement equation
steps.content:
The question provides the displacement of the object as a function of time:

$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$

steps.title: Find the amplitude
steps.content:
Using the **Analysis of Initial Conditions** approach, we first determine the amplitude ($A$) by finding the maximum possible value of the displacement function.

The cosine function, $\cos(\theta)$, oscillates between $-1$ and $+1$. Therefore, the maximum value of $\cos(5t)$ is $1$.

$$A = |0.15 \times 1| = 0.15\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Identify angular frequency
steps.content:
The angular frequency ($\omega$) determines how fast the cosine function oscillates. It is the coefficient of time ($t$) inside the cosine term.

Looking at the equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$:

$$\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Determine phase constant
steps.content:
To find the phase constant ($\phi$), we evaluate the displacement at the initial time $t=0$.

Substituting $t=0$ into the given equation:
$$x(0) = 0.15 \cos(5 \times 0) = 0.15 \cos(0)$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression:

calculator.description: Calculate displacement at t=0

steps.title: Calculate phase constant
steps.content:
Since $\cos(0) = 1$, the initial displacement is:

$$x(0) = 0.15\ \text{m}$$

We compare this to the general SHM equation with a phase constant: $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. At $t=0$:

$$0.15 = 0.15 \cos(\phi)$$
$$1 = \cos(\phi)$$

The angle whose cosine is $1$ is $0$. Therefore, the phase constant is:

$$\phi = 0$$

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
We have determined all the required parameters:

*   **Amplitude:** $A = 0.15\ \text{m}$
*   **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
*   **Phase Constant:** $\phi = 0$

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the mass of the object and the displacement equation from the question specification.

Given:
*   Mass $m = 0.30\ \text{kg}$
*   Displacement equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$

steps.title: Determine angular frequency
steps.content:
The general equation for simple harmonic motion (SHM) starting at maximum displacement is:

$$x = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

By comparing this with the given equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$, we can directly identify the angular frequency $\omega$ as the coefficient of time $t$:

$$\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: State the spring constant formula
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system undergoing simple harmonic motion, the restoring force is related to the angular frequency by the equation:

$$k = m\omega^2$$

This relationship is derived from combining Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$) with the definition of SHM ($a=-\omega^2 x$) and Hooke's Law ($F=-kx$). This concept is covered in **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Calculate the spring constant
steps.content:
Substitute the values of mass $m = 0.30\ \text{kg}$ and angular frequency $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$ into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} k &= 0.30 \times 5^2 \\ k &= 0.30 \times 25 \\ k &= 7.5\ \text{N m}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the time period $T$ of the oscillation using the relationship between period and angular frequency:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Substituting $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{5}\ \text{s}$$

steps.title: Apply spring period formula
steps.content:
We use the standard formula for the period of a mass-spring system, which relates the period $T$, mass $m$, and spring constant $k$:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

This formula is a key equation in **Topic C.1: Simple Harmonic Motion**. We can substitute our calculated expression for $T$ and the given mass $m=0.30\ \text{kg}$:

steps.title: Solve for spring constant
steps.content:
Now we equate the two expressions for the period and solve for $k$:

$$\frac{2\pi}{5} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.30}{k}}$$

Divide both sides by $2\pi$:

$$\frac{1}{5} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30}{k}}$$

Square both sides to remove the square root:

$$\frac{1}{25} = \frac{0.30}{k}$$

Rearrange to solve for $k$:

$$k = 0.30 \times 25$$

steps.title: Identify values from the equation
steps.content:
The displacement of the object is given by the equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$. By comparing this to the general equation for Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$, we can identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$).

$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= 0.15\ \text{m} \\ \omega &= 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1} \\ m &= 0.30\ \text{kg} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum acceleration
steps.content:
Using the identified values, we calculate the maximum acceleration of the object. The magnitude of maximum acceleration in SHM is given by $a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 x_0$. This relationship corresponds to the formula $$a = \omega^2 r$$ found in the data booklet, where the amplitude $x_0$ replaces the radius $r$.

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} a_{\text{max}} &= (5)^2 \times 0.15 \\ a_{\text{max}} &= 25 \times 0.15 \\ a_{\text{max}} &= 3.75\ \text{m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum restoring force
steps.content:
According to Newton's Second Law, the maximum restoring force $F_{\text{max}}$ acts on the mass when the acceleration is at its maximum. We use the formula:

$$F = ma$$

Substituting the mass and our calculated maximum acceleration:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{max}} &= 0.30 \times 3.75 \\ F_{\text{max}} &= 1.125\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for spring constant
steps.content:
Finally, we determine the spring constant $k$ using Hooke's Law. At the point of maximum displacement ($x = x_0$), the restoring force is at its maximum magnitude ($F_{\text{max}}$).

Using the magnitude form of Hooke's Law $F = kx$:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{max}} &= k x_0 \\ k &= \frac{F_{\text{max}}}{x_0} \\ k &= \frac{1.125}{0.15} \\ k &= 7.5\ \text{N m}^{-1} 
\end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify values from the question
steps.content:
First, we need to extract the physical quantities from the problem statement and the given displacement equation.

We are given the mass:
$$m = 0.30\ \text{kg}$$

The displacement equation is given in the form $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$:
$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$

Comparing this to the standard SHM equation, we can identify the amplitude ($x_0$ or $A$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$):
$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= 0.15\ \text{m} \\ \omega &= 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Select the energy formula
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the **maximum kinetic energy** corresponds to the **total energy** of the system. This occurs when the potential energy is zero (at the equilibrium position).

The formula for the total energy (and thus the maximum kinetic energy) in SHM depends on the mass, angular frequency, and amplitude:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x_0^2$$

This relationship is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our identified values directly into the energy equation:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.30 \times (5)^2 \times (0.15)^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Calculate the final value:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= 0.5 \times 0.30 \times 25 \times 0.0225 \\ &= 0.084375\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation:

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to 2 significant figures (consistent with the given data $0.30$ and $0.15$), the maximum kinetic energy is:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = 0.084\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
First, we compare the given displacement equation to the general equation for Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM): $$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$$ where $A$ is the amplitude and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.

From the given equation $$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$, we can identify:
- Amplitude $A = 0.15\ \text{m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.30\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Calculate maximum velocity
steps.content:
In SHM, the maximum velocity $v_{\text{max}}$ is related to the amplitude and angular frequency by the relationship:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega A$$

Substituting our values:

$$v_{\text{max}} = 5 \times 0.15 = 0.75\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

Now we use the standard formula for kinetic energy from the data booklet to find the maximum kinetic energy.

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Using the maximum velocity we just calculated:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= \frac{1}{2} m (v_{\text{max}})^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.30 \times (0.75)^2 \end{aligned}$$

Solving gives:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = 0.084375\ \text{J}$$

Rounding to 2 significant figures (consistent with the data provided):

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = 0.084\ \text{J}$$

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
First, we identify the physical quantities given in the question. We have the mass $m = 0.30\ \text{kg}$.

Comparing the given equation $x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$ with the standard SHM equation $x = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$ (or $x = A \cos(\omega t)$), we can identify:
- Amplitude $A = 0.15\ \text{m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the spring constant
steps.content:
In the "Using Spring Constant" method, we first determine the stiffness of the system. For a mass-spring system, the relationship between angular frequency, mass, and the spring constant $k$ is:

$$k = m\omega^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$k = 0.30 \times 5^2 = 0.30 \times 25 = 7.5\ \text{N m}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Use conservation of energy
steps.content:
Due to conservation of energy in simple harmonic motion, the maximum kinetic energy is equal to the maximum elastic potential energy stored in the spring. This occurs when the object is at its maximum displacement (amplitude $A$).

We calculate this using the potential energy formula:

$$E_{k(\text{max})} = E_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{1}{2}kA^2$$

steps.title: Calculate final energy
steps.content:
Substitute the values for $k$ and $A$ into the energy equation:

$$E_{k(\text{max})} = \frac{1}{2} \times 7.5 \times (0.15)^2$$

$$E_{k(\text{max})} = 3.75 \times 0.0225$$

$$E_{k(\text{max})} \approx 0.084\ \text{J}$$

parts.content: Determine the time when the object first passes through the equilibrium position moving in the negative direction.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognition that equilibrium occurs at $x = 0$: $5t = \frac{\pi}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Correct time: $t = \frac{\pi}{10}$ or $0.314\ \text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>At this time, the velocity $v(t) = -0.75\sin(5t)$ is $v(\frac{\pi}{10}) = -0.75\sin(\frac{\pi}{2}) = -0.75\ \text{m s}^{-1}$, which is in the negative direction.</note>

steps.title: Identify the equilibrium condition
steps.content:
The equilibrium position is defined as the point where the displacement is zero ($x=0$). To find when the object passes through this position, we must set the given displacement equation equal to zero.

$$x(t) = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the trigonometric equation
steps.content:
Substitute the displacement function given in the question into our equilibrium condition:

$$0.15 \cos(5t) = 0$$

steps.title: Determine the phase angle
steps.content:
We can divide both sides by 0.15 to isolate the cosine term:

$$\cos(5t) = 0$$

The cosine function equals zero at specific angles: $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \dots$. 

Since the motion starts at the maximum positive displacement ($t=0, x=0.15$) and moves towards zero, the **first** time it reaches the equilibrium position, it must be moving in the negative direction. This corresponds to the first angle where cosine is zero:

$$5t = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now, solve the equation for $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} 5t &= \frac{\pi}{2} \\ t &= \frac{\pi}{10} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type pi/10 and press EXE

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical value of pi/10

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Converting the fraction to a decimal gives the final time value.

$$t = \frac{\pi}{10} \text{ s} \approx 0.314 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
From the given equation for displacement, we can identify the angular frequency $\omega$ by comparing it to the standard simple harmonic motion equation $$x = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$:

$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$

Comparing the terms inside the cosine function, we find that $\omega = 5\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Calculate the time period
steps.content:
Using the angular frequency, we calculate the period of oscillation $T$. This represents the time taken for one complete cycle.

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{5}\ \text{s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi / 5

calculator.description: Calculate the period T

steps.title: Determine the fraction of the cycle
steps.content:
The object starts at $t=0$. Since the function is a cosine function ($x = 0.15 \cos(5t)$), the object starts at its maximum positive displacement ($+0.15\ \text{m}$). 

To reach the equilibrium position ($x=0$) for the **first** time, the object moves from the maximum amplitude to zero. This corresponds to one-quarter of a full cycle.

$$t = \frac{T}{4}$$

highlights.text: first passes through the equilibrium position

steps.title: Verify direction of motion
steps.content:
We must confirm this matches the requirement of "moving in the negative direction".

Since the object starts at a positive position ($+0.15\ \text{m}$) and moves towards equilibrium ($0\ \text{m}$), its displacement is decreasing. This means the velocity is negative. Therefore, at the first equilibrium passage ($t = \frac{T}{4}$), the object is indeed moving in the negative direction.

steps.title: Calculate the final time
steps.content:
Now we substitute our value for $T$ to find the time $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{1}{4} \times \left( \frac{2\pi}{5} \right) \\ &= \frac{2\pi}{20} \\ &= \frac{\pi}{10}\ \text{s} \end{aligned}$$

Converting to a decimal:
$$t \approx 0.314\ \text{s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter pi / 10

calculator.description: Calculate the final time t

steps.title: Find the velocity function
steps.content:
To apply the **Velocity Maximization** approach, we first need to determine the velocity function $v(t)$ by differentiating the given displacement equation $x(t)$ with respect to time.

Given the displacement equation:
$$x(t) = 0.15 \cos(5t)$$

The velocity is the derivative of displacement, $v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt}$. Using the chain rule $\frac{d}{dt}[\cos(u)] = -u'\sin(u)$:

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}[0.15 \cos(5t)] \\ &= 0.15 \times (-5 \sin(5t)) \\ &= -0.75 \sin(5t) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify condition for max negative velocity
steps.content:
The question asks for the time when the object passes through equilibrium **moving in the negative direction**. In Simple Harmonic Motion, the equilibrium position corresponds to the maximum speed. Since it is moving in the negative direction, we are looking for the **maximum negative velocity**.

For the function $v(t) = -0.75 \sin(5t)$, the maximum negative value occurs when the sine term is at its maximum positive value ($+1$), because of the negative sign in front:

$$v_{\text{max negative}} = -0.75(1) = -0.75 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

highlights.text: passes through the equilibrium position moving in the negative direction

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Now we set the sine component equal to $1$ to find the first time $t$ this occurs:

$$\sin(5t) = 1$$

The inverse sine of $1$ is $\frac{\pi}{2}$ radians. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned} 5t &= \frac{\pi}{2} \\ t &= \frac{\pi}{10} \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final numerical value
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the numerical value for the time $t$.

$$t = \frac{\pi}{10} \approx 0.314 \text{ s}$$

parts.content: Explain how the total energy of the system is related to both position and velocity.
parts.markscheme:
- State that total energy $E = \frac{1}{2}kA^2$ is constant <marks>A1</marks>
 - Relationship showing energy trade-off: $E = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State conservation of energy
steps.content:
According to the **Conservation of Energy Principle**, the total energy ($E_{total}$) of an oscillating system is constant (assuming no energy is lost to friction). This total energy is always the sum of the system's kinetic energy and potential energy at any given instant.

This concept is central to **Topic C.1.3: Energy transformations in oscillations**.

steps.title: Define kinetic energy
steps.content:
The kinetic energy ($E_k$) depends on the object's velocity ($v$) and mass ($m$). We use the standard formula found in the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

steps.title: Define potential energy
steps.content:
For a spring undergoing simple harmonic motion, the potential energy is stored as elastic potential energy ($E_p$). This depends on the displacement ($x$) from the equilibrium position and the spring constant ($k$):

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

steps.title: Combine into total energy equation
steps.content:
We can now write the equation relating total energy to both position and velocity by summing the two forms of energy:

$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

This equation shows that as displacement $x$ increases, velocity $v$ must decrease to keep $E_{total}$ constant.

steps.title: State the constant maximum energy
steps.content:
Since the total energy is constant, we can calculate it at the point of maximum displacement (the amplitude, $A$), where the object momentarily stops ($v=0$). Substituting $x=A$ and $v=0$ into our equation gives the constant value:

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}kA^2$$

This final expression represents the constant total energy of the system.

steps.title: Define total mechanical energy
steps.content:
The total energy ($E$) of a system undergoing Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) is the sum of its kinetic energy ($E_k$) and potential energy ($E_p$).

Using the standard formulas for kinetic energy and elastic potential energy:

$$E = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

steps.title: Use SHM kinematic relation
steps.content:
To express the energy entirely in terms of constants, we use the standard relationship between velocity ($v$) and position ($x$) for SHM:

$$v^2 = \omega^2(A^2 - x^2)$$

where $\omega$ is the angular frequency and $A$ is the amplitude.

steps.title: Substitute velocity into energy
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the expression for $v^2$ into the kinetic energy term. Recall that the spring constant is related to mass and frequency by $k = m\omega^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2}m(\omega^2(A^2 - x^2)) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}(m\omega^2)(A^2 - x^2) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}k(A^2 - x^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Derive the final energy equation
steps.content:
Finally, we add the kinetic and potential energy components together to find the total energy:

$$\begin{aligned} E &= E_k + E_p \\ &= \frac{1}{2}k(A^2 - x^2) + \frac{1}{2}kx^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2}kA^2 - \frac{1}{2}kx^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2}kA^2 \end{aligned}$$

This result shows that the total energy is constant and depends only on the spring constant ($k$) and the square of the amplitude ($A$).

questions.specification: A particle executes simple harmonic motion (SHM) with an amplitude of $$x_0$$ and a maximum speed of $$v_0$$. What is the speed of the particle at the instant its displacement from the equilibrium position is $$0.6x_0$$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the relationship between displacement and velocity in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM). This is a fundamental part of defining and analyzing the kinematics of SHM systems.

options.markscheme: This distractor is based on the incorrect linear subtraction of the displacement ratio from the maximum value, assuming $$v = v_0(1 - 0.6)$$. In SHM, the relationship between speed and displacement is non-linear and governed by the conservation of energy or trig identities.

This distractor arises when the student correctly identifies the term $$1 - (x/x_0)^2$$ from the velocity equation $$v = v_0\sqrt{1 - (x/x_0)^2}$$ but fails to take the square root of $$0.64$$ at the final step.

This distractor assumes a direct proportionality between speed and displacement ($$v \propto x$$), or simply replicates the value provided for the displacement ratio without applying the kinematic equations of SHM.

The relationship between speed $$v$$ and displacement $$x$$ in simple harmonic motion is given by $$v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$$. 
- The maximum speed $$v_0$$ occurs at the equilibrium position ($$x = 0$$), which gives $$v_0 = \omega x_0$$. 
- By substituting the given displacement $$x = 0.6x_0$$ into the equation: $$v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - (0.6x_0)^2}$$
- $$v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - 0.36x_0^2} = \omega \sqrt{0.64x_0^2}$$
- $$v = 0.8\omega x_0$$
- Since $$v_0 = \omega x_0$$, the speed is $$0.80 v_0$$.

steps.title: Identify the general equation
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we compare the given displacement equation to the standard general form for simple harmonic motion (SHM) found in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. 

The general equation for displacement is:
$$x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $x_0$ is the amplitude
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency
- $\phi$ is the phase angle
- $t$ is time

steps.title: Identify the amplitude
steps.content:
By looking at the given equation and the general form side-by-side:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \mathbf{0.15} \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right) \\ x &= \mathbf{x_0} \sin(\omega t + \phi) \end{aligned}$$

The coefficient in front of the sine function represents the amplitude. 

$$x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
Next, we look at the coefficient of the time variable $t$ inside the sine function:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 0.15 \sin \left( \mathbf{5} t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right) \\ x &= x_0 \sin(\mathbf{\omega} t + \phi) \end{aligned}$$

Matching these terms gives us the angular frequency:

$$\omega = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the phase angle
steps.content:
Finally, we identify the constant term added to the time-dependent term within the parentheses:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \mathbf{\frac{\pi}{6}} \right) \\ x &= x_0 \sin(\omega t + \mathbf{\phi}) \end{aligned}$$

This constant is the phase angle:

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Graph the SHM function
steps.content:
To analyze the motion graphically, we first input the displacement function into the graphing calculator. Ensure your calculator is set to **radians mode**, as the phase angle and angular frequency are given in radians.

Input the function:
$$y = 0.15 \sin\left(5x + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$
where $y$ represents displacement $x$ and $x$ represents time $t$. Adjust the window settings to see at least one full cycle (e.g., $x_{min} = 0, x_{max} = 2$).

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to Radians. In the graphing menu, enter the function f(x) = 0.15 sin(5x + π/6). View the graph in a window from x: [0, 2] and y: [-0.2, 0.2].

ti84.instructions: Press [MODE], select RADIANS. Press [Y=], enter 0.15*sin(5X + π/6). Press [GRAPH]. Adjust [WINDOW] to Xmin=0, Xmax=2, Ymin=-0.2, Ymax=0.2.

desmos.instructions: Ensure Desmos is in Radians mode. Type the equation: y = 0.15 sin(5x + pi/6).

calculator.description: Graph the displacement function to visualize the oscillation.

steps.title: Determine the amplitude
steps.content:
The **amplitude** ($x_0$) is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position. On the graph, this corresponds to the $y$-value of the peaks.

Using the GDC's 'Maximum' tool, we find that the highest point on the curve is $0.15$.

$$x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the 'G-Solve' or 'Analyze Graph' function and select 'Maximum'.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [CALC], select '4: maximum'. Move the cursor to the left and right of a peak to set boundaries, then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click on any peak of the graphed sine wave to see its coordinates. The y-coordinate is the amplitude.

calculator.description: Find the maximum y-value of the graph.

steps.title: Determine the angular frequency
steps.content:
The **angular frequency** ($\omega$) is related to the period ($T$) of the motion. The period is the time taken for one complete oscillation (the horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks).

1. Use the GDC to find the $x$-coordinates of two consecutive peaks. 
2. Calculate the difference: $T = t_2 - t_1 \approx 1.2566 \text{ s}$.
3. Calculate $\omega$ using the relation:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{1.2566} = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the x-distance between two peaks to get the period T. Then calculate 2π/T on the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Find two consecutive maximums. Subtract the x-values to find T. Then calculate 2*π / T.

desmos.instructions: Identify the x-values of two adjacent peaks (e.g., 0.209 and 1.466). Calculate T = 1.466 - 0.209 = 1.257. Then calculate 2π / 1.257.

calculator.description: Calculate the period and then the angular frequency.

steps.title: Identify the phase angle
steps.content:
The **phase angle** ($\phi$) represents the starting point of the oscillation at $t = 0$. In the general equation for SHM:
$$x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

By comparing the given equation $x = 0.15 \sin(5t + \frac{\pi}{6})$ to the general form, we can directly read the value added to the time-dependent term.

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
Based on our graphical analysis and comparison with the standard SHM equation, the values are:

- **Amplitude**: $x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- **Angular frequency**: $\omega = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- **Phase angle**: $\phi = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$

Each of these matches the required components for the 3 marks.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum velocity of the particle during its motion.
parts.markscheme:
$v_{\max} = \omega x_0$

$v_{\max} = 5 \times 0.15$ <marks>M1</marks>

$v_{\max} = 0.75 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Extract SHM parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the direct SHM formula, we first compare the given displacement equation to the standard general form of simple harmonic motion:

$$\text{General: } x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$
$$\text{Given: } x = 0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right)$$

By comparing the two, we can identify:
- The amplitude ($x_0$) is $0.15\text{ m}$.
- The angular frequency ($\omega$) is $5\text{ rad s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Identify the maximum velocity formula
steps.content:
In Simple Harmonic Motion (Topic C.1), the maximum velocity ($v_{\text{max}}$) of the particle occurs as it passes through the equilibrium position. It is calculated using the product of the angular frequency and the amplitude:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

This formula is a standard relationship derived from the kinematics of SHM.

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values we identified in Step 0 into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 5 \times 0.15 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 0.75 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, showing this substitution earns the first mark (M1), and the final correct answer with units earns the second mark (A1).

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum velocity by multiplying angular frequency and amplitude.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The maximum velocity of the particle is $0.75 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Define velocity using calculus
steps.content:
In physics, the velocity $v$ of a particle is defined as the rate of change of its displacement $x$ with respect to time $t$. We can find the velocity function by differentiating the given displacement equation:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the displacement function
steps.content:
We are given the displacement function:

$$x(t) = 0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right)$$

To find the derivative, we apply the chain rule. The derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u)$, and we must also multiply by the derivative of the expression inside the parentheses ($5t + \frac{\pi}{6}$), which is $5$:

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= \frac{d}{dt} \left[ 0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right) \right] \\ v(t) &= 0.15 \times 5 \times \cos \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify the velocity expression
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the coefficient of the cosine function. This value represents the maximum possible value of the velocity.

$$\begin{aligned} v(t) &= 0.75 \cos \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right) \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.15 * 5.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of the amplitude and the angular frequency.

steps.title: Identify the maximum velocity
steps.content:
The maximum value of a cosine function is $1$. Therefore, the maximum velocity $v_{\text{max}}$ is simply the amplitude of the velocity function:

$$v_{\text{max}} = 0.75 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$$

This matches the $v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$ relationship used in the markscheme, where $\omega = 5 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$ and $x_0 = 0.15 \, \text{m}$.

parts.content:
The mass of the particle is $0.50 \, \mathrm{kg}$.

Calculate the total energy of the particle in this simple harmonic motion.
parts.markscheme:
$E_T = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x_0^2$

$E_T = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.50 \times 5^2 \times 0.15^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

$E_T = 0.14 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To calculate the total energy, we first need to identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. The standard form for SHM displacement is:

$$x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Comparing this to the given equation $x=0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right)$, we can extract:
- Amplitude, $x_0 = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Angular frequency, $\omega = 5 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Apply total energy formula
steps.content:
The total energy ($E_T$) of a system undergoing simple harmonic motion is constant and can be calculated using the mass, angular frequency, and amplitude. This concept is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

The formula for total energy is:

$$E_T = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x_0^2$$

From the question, we know the mass of the particle is $0.50 \text{ kg}$. Substituting our values into the formula gives:

$$E_T = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.50 \times 5^2 \times 0.15^2$$

highlights.text: mass of the particle is 0.50 kg

steps.title: Calculate and round result
steps.content:
Now, we perform the calculation. This step typically earns the final mark in an IB physics paper.

$$\begin{aligned} E_T &= 0.25 \times 25 \times 0.0225 \\ E_T &= 0.140625 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (matching the precision of the given data), we get:

$$E_T = 0.14 \text{ J}$$
