steps.title: Identify SHM Parameters
steps.content:
To find the total energy, we first need to extract the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation:

$$x = 0.15 \sin \left( 5 t + \frac{\pi}{6} \right)$$

Comparing this to the standard SHM equation $x = x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, we can see:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.15 \, \text{m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$
- Mass $m = 0.50 \, \text{kg}$

highlights.text: The mass of the particle is $0.50 \, \mathrm{kg}$

steps.title: Calculate Maximum Velocity
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the maximum velocity ($v_{\text{max}}$) occurs as the particle passes through the equilibrium position. It is calculated using the relationship between angular frequency and amplitude:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 5 \times 0.15 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 0.75 \, \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 5 * 0.15 on the calculator screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 5 * 0.15 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum velocity.

steps.title: Determine Total Energy
steps.content:
The total energy ($E_T$) in SHM is constant and is equal to the maximum kinetic energy (when the particle is at the equilibrium position and potential energy is zero). 

We use the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Substituting the mass and our calculated maximum velocity:

$$\begin{aligned} E_T &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.50 \times (0.75)^2 \\ E_T &= 0.25 \times 0.5625 \\ E_T &= 0.140625 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get $E_T = 0.14 \, \text{J}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.5 * 0.75^2 on the calculator screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.5 * 0.5 * 0.75^2 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy using the kinetic energy formula.

parts.content: When the displacement of the particle is $x=0.10 \, \mathrm{m}$, calculate the potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle.
parts.markscheme:
$E_P = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2$

$E_P = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.50 \times 5^2 \times 0.10^2 = 0.0625 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$E_K = E_T - E_P = 0.14 - 0.0625$ <marks>M1</marks>

$E_K = 0.078 \, \mathrm{J}$ (Allow $0.0775 \, \mathrm{J}$) <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A mass of $0.25 \text{ kg}$ is attached to a horizontal spring on a frictionless surface. The mass is displaced and then released so that it undergoes simple harmonic motion. The following table shows the magnitude of the restoring force $F$ acting on the mass at various displacements $x$ from the equilibrium position.

| Displacement $x$ / $10^{-2} \text{ m}$ | Restoring Force $F$ / $\text{N}$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| $2.0$ | $0.50$ |
| $4.0$ | $1.00$ |
| $6.0$ | $1.50$ |
| $8.0$ | $2.00$ |

parts.content: Define angular frequency $(\omega)$ and explain how it is related to the time period and frequency of a particle undergoing simple harmonic motion.
parts.markscheme:
- Define angular frequency: The rate of change of phase angle with respect to time. <marks>A1</marks>
- State relationship with frequency: $\omega=2 \pi f$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State relationship with time period: $\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define angular frequency
steps.content:
In physics, specifically within **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, angular frequency ($\omega$) is defined analytically as the rate at which the phase angle of an oscillating system changes over time. 

If we consider the phase angle $\phi$ in radians, the angular frequency is:
$$\omega = \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}$$

This earns the first mark as it identifies the relationship between the phase change and time.

steps.title: Relationship with frequency
steps.content:
One full oscillation corresponds to a change in the phase angle of $2\pi$ radians. Since the frequency ($f$) represents the number of complete oscillations per unit time, the total phase change per second is $2\pi$ times the frequency.

The mathematical relationship is:
$$\omega = 2\pi f$$

This formula is a standard definition used throughout the IB syllabus to convert linear frequency (in Hertz) to angular frequency (in radians per second).

steps.title: Relationship with time period
steps.content:
The time period ($T$) is the time taken for one complete oscillation ($2\pi$ radians). Because frequency and time period are inversely related ($f = \frac{1}{T}$), we can substitute this into our previous equation to find the relationship with the time period:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

In the IB exam, stating both of these mathematical relationships clearly earns the remaining two marks.

steps.title: Link SHM to Circular Motion
steps.content:
To understand angular frequency, we can imagine a particle moving in a circle with constant speed. If we look at the projection (the "shadow") of this particle on a straight line, that shadow moves with **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. 

One complete cycle of the SHM oscillation corresponds exactly to one full revolution of the particle around the reference circle. Since a full circle covers an angle of $2\pi$ radians, we use this "phase angle" to describe where the particle is in its cycle.

steps.title: Define Angular Frequency
steps.content:
In the context of the reference circle, the **angular frequency** $(\omega)$ is defined as the rate at which the phase angle changes over time. 

As the particle moves around the circle, the angle $\theta$ increases. The speed of this increase is:

$$\omega = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$$

In the IB markscheme, this is specifically defined as the **rate of change of phase angle with respect to time**.

highlights.text: Define angular frequency: The rate of change of phase angle with respect to time.

steps.title: Relate to Frequency
steps.content:
The frequency $(f)$ is the number of complete oscillations (or full rotations) per second. 

Since each single rotation covers $2\pi$ radians, if the particle completes $f$ rotations in one second, the total angle covered is $2\pi \times f$. Therefore, the angular frequency is:

$$\omega = 2\pi f$$

steps.title: Relate to Time Period
steps.content:
The time period $(T)$ is the time taken for one complete oscillation. 

In the circular motion model, the particle travels $2\pi$ radians in exactly one period $T$. Using the formula for angular speed (angle divided by time), we get:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

This shows that angular frequency is inversely proportional to the time period.

parts.content:
A simple pendulum has a length of $2.0 \text{ m}$.

Calculate the time period of the pendulum for small oscillations.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the formula for time period: $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{2.0}{9.8}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the time period: $T=2.84 \text{ s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To solve this, we first look at the values provided in the question. We are given the length of the pendulum, and we know the standard value for acceleration due to gravity on Earth from the Physical Constants section of the data booklet.

- Length ($L$) = $2.0 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Select the period formula
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum undergoing small oscillations, we use the time period formula found in **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

Where:
- $T$ is the time period (s)
- $L$ is the length (m)
- $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$)

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values directly into the formula to find $T$:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2.0}{9.8}}$$

Substituting the values correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Use the square root and pi functions to calculate 2 * π * √(2.0 / 9.8).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time period using the pendulum formula.

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$T \approx 2.83845... \text{ s}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (as per the markscheme), we get the final answer:

$$T = 2.84 \text{ s}$$

Providing the final numerical answer with units earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the time period of the pendulum, we first identify the known values from the question and the data booklet. We are given the length of the pendulum and we need the acceleration due to gravity.

- Length ($L$) = $2.0 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum undergoing small oscillations, the angular frequency ($\omega$) is related to the length and gravity by the formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{9.8}{2.0}} = \sqrt{4.9}$$

$$\omega \approx 2.21 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and input the fraction 9.8 over 2.0.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of the ratio between gravity and length.

steps.title: Calculate the time period
steps.content:
The time period ($T$) is the time taken for one full oscillation. It is inversely related to the angular frequency by the following fundamental relationship:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Using our calculated value for $\omega$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{4.9}}$$

$$T \approx 2.8384... \text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 times pi by the result of the previous calculation.

calculator.description: Divide 2π by the previously calculated angular frequency.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding the result to two decimal places (consistent with the provided data), we get:

$$T = 2.84 \text{ s}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution (even if using the combined formula $T=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$) and one mark for the correct final answer with units.

parts.content: Determine the angular frequency of the pendulum. Use $g=9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
parts.markscheme:

- Calculate the angular frequency: $\omega=2.21 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept calculation using $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$ which gives the same result.</note>

steps.title: Recall the angular frequency formula
steps.content:
To find the angular frequency ($\omega$) using the period ($T$), we use the fundamental relationship derived from simple harmonic motion (SHM). This concept is covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Where:
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency (in $\text{rad s}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is the time period of one full oscillation (in $\text{s}$)

steps.title: Identify the period
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the question, the period of the pendulum was determined to be:

$$T = 2.84 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Calculate the angular frequency
steps.content:
Substitute the value of the period into the angular frequency formula:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{2.84}$$

Using a calculator to solve this gives us the final value.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2π / 2.84 and press execute.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] (for π), multiply by 2, then divide by 2.84. Press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency by dividing 2π by the period.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$\omega = 2.21 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

In the markscheme, the first mark (**M1**) is awarded for the correct substitution, and the second mark (**A1**) is for the final numerical answer with units.

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To determine the angular frequency $\omega$ using the physical properties of the pendulum, we use the relationship between gravity $g$ and the length of the pendulum $L$. 

In **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, we learn that for a simple pendulum, the angular frequency is given by:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

This method is a direct way to find $\omega$ if you already know the length of the pendulum from a previous part of the problem.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Using the provided value for acceleration due to gravity and the pendulum length ($L = 2.00 \text{ m}$), we substitute them into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \sqrt{\frac{9.8}{2.00}} \\ \omega &\approx 2.21359... \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $\omega = 2.21 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Calculator Verification
steps.content: You can quickly calculate this on your GDC to ensure accuracy.

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function, then input the fraction 9.8 over 2.00.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (for square root), then type 9.8 / 2.00 and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate angular frequency from gravity and length.

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
The angular frequency of the pendulum is:

$$\omega = 2.21 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

**Marking Note:** 
- One mark (**M1**) is awarded for the correct substitution into the formula.
- One mark (**A1**) is awarded for the final value with the correct units.
- Even though the markscheme shows the calculation using period $T$, the note confirms that using the length formula is equally valid and yields the same result.

parts.content: Determine the displacement and velocity of the particle at $t = 0.25\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme:
$x = 0.20 \cdot \cos(8 \cdot 0.25 - \frac{\pi}{6}) = 0.20 \cdot \cos(2 - \frac{\pi}{6}) = 0.20 \cdot \cos(1.47) = 0.020\ \text{m}$
$v = -\omega x_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi) = -8 \cdot 0.20 \cdot \sin(1.47) = -1.60 \cdot 0.995 = -1.59\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
Correct $x$ 1 mark , correct $v$ 1 mark , correct trigonometric handling 1 mark

parts.content: Calculate the maximum speed of the particle.
parts.markscheme: $v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0 = 8 \cdot 0.20 = 1.60\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ 1 mark

parts.content: Determine the total energy of the system if the mass of the particle is $0.10\ \text{kg}$.
parts.markscheme: $E_T = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x_0^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 64 \cdot (0.20)^2 = 0.128\ \text{J}$ 1 mark Correct final answer 1 mark

parts.content: Calculate the potential energy of the particle at $t = 0.25\ \text{s}$.
parts.markscheme: $E_p = \frac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 64 \cdot (0.020)^2 = 0.00128\ \text{J}$ 1 mark Correct value with units 1 mark

parts.content: Explain the meaning of negative velocity in this context.
parts.markscheme: A negative velocity indicates the particle is moving in the opposite direction to the positive displacement axis (towards equilibrium). 1 mark

questions.specification: A block of mass $0.3 \, \text{kg}$ attached to a spring oscillates with a maximum speed of $1.2 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$. The spring constant is $20 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$, and this setup is used to test the limits of robotic movements.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts concern a mass–spring simple harmonic oscillator: finding amplitude from maximum speed, relating amplitude to maximum acceleration (a_max = ω^2 A), and computing maximum acceleration.

parts.content: Determine the amplitude of the oscillation.
parts.markscheme:
**Method 1 (Energy):**
- **Calculate** the kinetic energy: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.3)(1.2)^2 = 0.216 \, \text{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Equate** maximum kinetic energy to maximum potential energy: $0.216 = \frac{1}{2}kx_0^2 \Rightarrow 0.216 = \frac{1}{2}(20)x_0^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the amplitude: $x_0 = \sqrt{0.0216} \approx 0.147 \, \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Method 2 (Angular Frequency):**
- **Calculate** the angular frequency: $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.3}} \approx 8.16 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Substitute** into the amplitude formula: $x_0 = \frac{v_{\text{max}}}{\omega} = \frac{1.2}{8.16}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the amplitude: $x_0 \approx 0.147 \, \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the known values from the problem. We have the mass of the block, the maximum speed it reaches during oscillation, and the spring constant of the system.

- Mass $m = 0.3 \text{ kg}$
- Maximum speed $v_{\text{max}} = 1.2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- Spring constant $k = 20 \text{ N m}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the kinetic energy is at its maximum when the block passes through the equilibrium position. We use the kinetic energy formula from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.3)(1.2)^2$$

$$E_{k, \text{max}} = 0.216 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 0.3 * 1.2^2 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum kinetic energy.

steps.title: Equate energy at amplitude
steps.content:
According to the principle of **Conservation of Energy** (covered in **Topic A.3**), the maximum kinetic energy is equal to the maximum elastic potential energy at the amplitude ($x_0$). 

The formula for elastic potential energy in a spring is:

$$E_{p, \text{max}} = \frac{1}{2}kx_0^2$$

Setting these equal to each other:

$$0.216 = \frac{1}{2}(20)x_0^2$$

steps.title: Solve for the amplitude
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to find the amplitude $x_0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.216 &= 10x_0^2 \\ x_0^2 &= \frac{0.216}{10} \\ x_0^2 &= 0.0216 \\ x_0 &= \sqrt{0.0216} \end{aligned}$$

$$x_0 \approx 0.147 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter √0.0216 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd, then x² (for √), enter 0.0216, and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 0.0216 to find the amplitude.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The amplitude of the oscillation is **$0.147 \text{ m}$**. 

In an IB exam, you would earn marks for:
- Correct calculation of $E_k$ (**M1**)
- Correct energy conservation setup (**M1**)
- Final answer with correct significant figures and units (**A1**)

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the angular frequency approach, let's first list the information provided in the question:

*   Mass of the block ($m$): $0.3 \, \text{kg}$
*   Maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$): $1.2 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$
*   Spring constant ($k$): $20 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the angular frequency $\omega$ is determined by the spring constant and the mass. We use the following relationship found in **Topic C.1 (Simple Harmonic Motion)**:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.3}} \approx 8.165 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function, then 20 divided by 0.3.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency using the square root of k/m.

steps.title: Relate velocity to amplitude
steps.content:
In SHM, the maximum velocity occurs at the equilibrium position and is related to the amplitude ($x_0$) and angular frequency ($\omega$) by the equation:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

To find the amplitude, we rearrange the formula to solve for $x_0$:

$$x_0 = \frac{v_{\text{max}}}{\omega}$$

steps.title: Solve for amplitude
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values we have into the rearranged formula:

$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= \frac{1.2}{8.1649...} \\ x_0 &\approx 0.147 \, \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, the final answer is approximately $0.147 \, \text{m}$ (or $14.7 \, \text{cm}$).

gdc.instructions: Divide 1.2 by the value of omega calculated in the previous step.

calculator.description: Divide the maximum speed by the previously calculated angular frequency.

parts.content: Explain how the amplitude affects the maximum acceleration in SHM.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** the relationship between maximum acceleration and amplitude: $a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 x_0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Conclude** that maximum acceleration is directly proportional to amplitude: Since $\omega$ is constant, $a_{\text{max}} \propto x_0$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept logical explanations stating that larger amplitude results in greater restoring force and thus greater acceleration.</note>

steps.title: State the SHM acceleration equation
steps.content:
To explain the relationship, we start with the defining equation for acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM). This fundamental definition connects acceleration $a$, angular frequency $\omega$, and displacement $x$:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

The negative sign indicates that acceleration acts in the opposite direction to displacement (restoring force), but for this question, we are interested in the magnitude.

steps.title: Define maximum acceleration
steps.content:
The maximum acceleration $a_{\text{max}}$ occurs when the displacement is at its maximum value. The maximum displacement is defined as the **amplitude**, denoted as $x_0$.

Substituting $x_0$ into the magnitude of the equation:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 x_0$$

steps.title: Conclude the relationship
steps.content:
In a given mass-spring system, the angular frequency $\omega$ is determined by the system's physical properties (mass $m$ and spring constant $k$) and remains constant regardless of the amplitude.

Since $\omega^2$ is constant, we can conclude that maximum acceleration is **directly proportional** to the amplitude:

$$a_{\text{max}} \propto x_0$$

steps.title: Identify maximum force position
steps.content: To find the maximum acceleration using forces, we first consider where the force is greatest. In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the restoring force is maximum when the object is at its maximum displacement from equilibrium, which is the amplitude $x_0$.

steps.title: Apply Hooke's Law
steps.content:
Using Hooke's Law, the magnitude of the restoring force $F$ is proportional to the displacement. At the amplitude $x_0$, the maximum force is:

$$F_{\text{max}} = k x_0$$

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
According to **Newton's Second Law** found in the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet, force is directly related to acceleration:

$$F = ma$$

Substituting the maximum force into this equation:

$$m a_{\text{max}} = k x_0$$

steps.title: Solve for acceleration
steps.content:
We can rearrange the equation to solve for the maximum acceleration $a_{\text{max}}$:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \frac{k}{m} x_0$$

This equation connects the acceleration directly to the physical properties of the system ($k$ and $m$) and the amplitude.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Since the spring constant $k$ and the mass $m$ remain constant, the maximum acceleration depends directly on the amplitude. 

Therefore, maximum acceleration is **directly proportional** to the amplitude:

$$a_{\text{max}} \propto x_0$$

This means if you double the amplitude, the restoring force doubles, leading to double the maximum acceleration.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum acceleration of the block.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** known values into the acceleration formula: $a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 x_0 = \left(\frac{20}{0.3}\right)(0.147)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the maximum acceleration: $a_{\text{max}} \approx 9.80 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct answer using alternative methods (e.g., $a_{\text{max}} = \omega v_{\text{max}}$).</note>

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system in simple harmonic motion (Topic C.1), the angular frequency $\omega$ is defined by the spring constant and mass:

$$ \omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ \omega = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.3}} \approx 8.165 \, \text{rad s}^{-1} $$

steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the maximum acceleration $a_{\text{max}}$ is related to the maximum speed $v_{\text{max}}$ by the angular frequency:

$$ a_{\text{max}} = \omega v_{\text{max}} $$

Using our calculated value for $\omega$:

$$ a_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.3}} \times 1.2 $$

Computing the final value:

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
First, let's list the information given in the question. This problem relates to **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**.

We have:
- Mass $$m = 0.3 \, \text{kg}$$
- Maximum speed $$v_{\text{max}} = 1.2 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$$
- Spring constant $$k = 20 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system, the angular frequency $$\omega$$ is determined by the spring constant and the mass. We use the fundamental definition:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.3}} \approx 8.165 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate the amplitude
steps.content:
In simple harmonic motion, the maximum speed $$v_{\text{max}}$$ is related to the amplitude $$x_0$$ by the equation:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

Rearranging for $$x_0$$:

$$x_0 = \frac{v_{\text{max}}}{\omega} = \frac{1.2}{8.165} \approx 0.147 \, \text{m}$$

steps.title: Solve for maximum acceleration
steps.content:
Now we can use the standard acceleration formula for simple harmonic motion. The magnitude of maximum acceleration is given by:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \omega^2 x_0$$

Substituting our calculated values:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \left(\frac{20}{0.3}\right)(0.147)$$

$$a_{\text{max}} \approx 66.67 \times 0.147 \approx 9.80 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$$

This matches the method shown in the markscheme.

The maximum acceleration of the block is:

$$a_{\text{max}} = 9.80 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$$

steps.title: Strategy: Energy and Forces
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Energy and Forces** approach, we will follow three main stages:
1.  **Energy**: Equate the maximum kinetic energy to the maximum elastic potential energy to find the amplitude ($x_0$).
2.  **Force**: Use the amplitude to calculate the maximum restoring force using Hooke's Law ($F=kx$).
3.  **Acceleration**: Use Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$) to find the maximum acceleration.

steps.title: Calculate maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
The block has maximum speed $v_{\text{max}}$ as it passes through the equilibrium position. We calculate the maximum kinetic energy using the formula from the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

Substituting the given values $m = 0.3 \, \text{kg}$ and $v_{\text{max}} = 1.2 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$:

$$E_{k,\text{max}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.3)(1.2)^{2}$$

calculator.description: Calculate maximum kinetic energy

steps.title: Solve for amplitude
steps.content:
At maximum displacement (the amplitude $x_0$), all this kinetic energy has been converted into elastic potential energy ($E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$). By conservation of energy:

$$\frac{1}{2}kx_0^2 = 0.216 \, \text{J}$$

We solve for $x_0$ using the spring constant $k = 20 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 20 x_0^2 &= 2(0.216) \\ x_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{0.432}{20}} \\ x_0 &\approx 0.147 \, \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type: sqrt(0.432 / 20)

calculator.description: Solve for amplitude x0

steps.title: Calculate maximum force
steps.content:
Now we find the maximum restoring force exerted by the spring at this amplitude using Hooke's Law magnitude ($F = kx$):

$$F_{\text{max}} = k x_0$$

Substituting our values:

$$F_{\text{max}} = 20 \times 0.1469... \approx 2.94 \, \text{N}$$

gdc.instructions: Type: 20 * Ans

calculator.description: Calculate maximum force

steps.title: Determine maximum acceleration
steps.content:
Finally, we apply Newton's Second Law from the **Mechanics** section to find the acceleration:

$$F = ma \implies a_{\text{max}} = \frac{F_{\text{max}}}{m}$$

Substituting the force and mass:

$$a_{\text{max}} = \frac{2.94}{0.3} = 9.80 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$$

gdc.instructions: Type: Ans / 0.3

calculator.description: Calculate final acceleration

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The maximum acceleration of the block is:

$$a_{\text{max}} = 9.80 \, \text{m s}^{-2}$$

*(Note: This matches the result you would get using the alternative formula $a_{\text{max}} = \omega v_{\text{max}}$)*

questions.specification: In a robotics lab, a mass of $0.5 \, \text{kg}$ is attached to a spring with a spring constant of $40 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$ on a frictionless surface. The mass is displaced by $0.1 \, \text{m}$ and released from rest to test the oscillatory behavior of robotic arms.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) ask for angular frequency, period, and frequency of a mass–spring oscillator, which directly tests time period/frequency relations in SHM (ω=√(k/m), T=2π/ω, f=1/T).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.1 Defining and analyzing simple harmonic motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The setup is explicitly a frictionless mass–spring system undergoing simple harmonic motion, and part (c) uses the SHM result for maximum speed (v_max=ωA), which relies on core SHM definitions/analysis.

parts.content: Calculate the angular frequency of the system.
parts.markscheme:
### Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.5}} \\
&= \sqrt{80}
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

$$
\omega = 8.94 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award A1 for correct substitution into formula and A1 for correct working and answer.</note>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To find the angular frequency, we first need to identify the mass ($m$) and the spring constant ($k$) from the problem. This problem falls under **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, which explores the relationship between mass, restoring forces, and oscillation frequency.

steps.title: Select the formula
steps.content:
For a mass-spring system in Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the angular frequency ($\omega$) is directly determined by the stiffness of the spring and the inertia of the mass. 

The relationship is given by the formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into our equation. 

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.5}} \\ \omega &= \sqrt{80} \end{aligned}$$

Performing this calculation gives us the numerical value for the angular frequency.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression sqrt(40/0.5) in the main calculation window.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (for the square root symbol), then type 40 / 0.5 and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency by dividing the spring constant by the mass and taking the square root.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
The result of the calculation is approximately $8.944...$. 

In IB Physics, we typically round our final answer to 3 significant figures unless otherwise specified. Therefore, the angular frequency is:

$$\omega = 8.94 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the angular frequency, we first identify the properties of the mass-spring system from the question. We have a mass and a spring constant.

- Mass $m = 0.5 \, \text{kg}$
- Spring constant $k = 40 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the time period
steps.content:
In this approach, we first calculate the time period $T$ of the oscillation. This relates the mass and the spring constant as covered in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

Using the formula for a mass-spring system:
$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.5}{40}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi * sqrt(0.5 / 40) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] (for pi). Press [2] [*] [2nd] [^] [*] [2nd] [x²] (for sqrt). Enter [0.5] [/] [40]. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time period T numerically.

steps.title: Determine time period value
steps.content:
Evaluating the square root and multiplying by $2\pi$, we find:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 2\pi\sqrt{0.0125} \\ T &\approx 0.7025 \, \text{s} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Relate T to omega
steps.content:
Now that we have the time period $T$, we can find the angular frequency $\omega$. The relationship between angular frequency and time period is a fundamental definition in circular motion and oscillations:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substitute the value of $T$ we just calculated:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{0.7025...}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 * pi by the previously calculated result.

desmos.instructions: Divide 2pi by the value of T calculated in the previous step.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency using the previously found T.

steps.title: State final angular frequency
steps.content:
Performing the division gives us the final result. In IB exams, remember to round to an appropriate number of significant figures (usually 3).

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= 8.9442... \\ \omega &\approx 8.94 \, \text{rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the result we would get from the alternative direct formula $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$.

parts.content: Determine the period and frequency of the oscillation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method

$$
T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{8.94} = 0.702 \, \text{s}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.702} = 1.42 \, \text{Hz}
$$
<marks>A2</marks>

<note>Award A1 for correct substitution to find period, A2 for correct working and answers for period and frequency.</note>

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the period and frequency, we first need to extract the mass and the spring constant from the problem description. These values are essential for describing any simple harmonic oscillator.

*   Mass ($m$) = $0.5 \, \text{kg}$
*   Spring constant ($k$) = $40 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
In **Topic C.1: Simple Harmonic Motion**, we learn that the time period ($T$) for a mass-spring system depends only on the mass and the stiffness of the spring. We use the direct period formula:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.5}{40}}$$

$$T \approx 0.702 \, \text{s}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 2 * pi * sqrt(0.5 / 40).

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * pi * sqrt(0.5 / 40) into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the period T using the square root of the mass-to-spring-constant ratio.

steps.title: Calculate the frequency
steps.content:
The frequency ($f$) is the number of oscillations per second. It is the reciprocal of the period ($T$). The relationship is defined as:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

Using the period we just calculated:

$$f = \frac{1}{0.70248...}$$

$$f \approx 1.42 \, \text{Hz}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 divided by the previously found period.

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency f by taking the reciprocal of the period T.

steps.title: Final results and marks
steps.content:
Based on our calculations, the oscillation properties are:

*   **Period ($T$):** $0.702 \, \text{s}$ (1 mark for correct substitution and result)
*   **Frequency ($f$):** $1.42 \, \text{Hz}$ (2 marks for the correct working and final frequency value)

Note that frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz), which is equivalent to inverse seconds ($s^{-1}$).

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the period and frequency, we first need to identify the mass ($m$) and the spring constant ($k$) from the question.

*   Mass $m = 0.5 \, \text{kg}$
*   Spring constant $k = 40 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
Using the angular frequency approach, we start by calculating $\omega$ (angular frequency). For a mass-spring system, this is given by the relationship:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.5}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944 \, \text{rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Type sqrt(40/0.5) into the main calculation screen and press exe.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(40/0.5) in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency using the square root function.

steps.title: Determine the time period
steps.content:
Now we can find the time period ($T$). The relationship between period and angular frequency is defined as:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Substituting the value of $\omega$ we just calculated:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{8.944} \approx 0.702 \, \text{s}$$

This earns the first marks for correct substitution and result for the period.

gdc.instructions: Type (2*pi)/8.944 and press exe.

desmos.instructions: Type 2*pi/8.944 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the time period using the value of pi and the calculated angular frequency.

steps.title: Calculate the frequency
steps.content:
Finally, we find the frequency ($f$). Frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

Using our period value:

$$f = \frac{1}{0.702} \approx 1.42 \, \text{Hz}$$

Alternatively, using the angular frequency directly: $f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{8.944}{2\pi} \approx 1.42 \, \text{Hz}$.

gdc.instructions: Type 1/0.702 and press exe.

desmos.instructions: Type 1/0.702 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency by taking the reciprocal of the period.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The oscillatory behavior of the mass is characterized by:

*   **Period ($T$):** $0.702 \, \text{s}$
*   **Frequency ($f$):** $1.42 \, \text{Hz}$

parts.content: Find the maximum speed of the mass during oscillation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{max}} &= \omega x_0 \\
&= 8.94 \times 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

$$
v_{\text{max}} = 0.894 \, \text{m s}^{-1}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award A1 for correct substitution into formula and A1 for correct working and answer.</note>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the maximum speed using Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) kinematics, we first need to list the known values from the problem statement:

- Mass ($m$) = $0.5 \, \text{kg}$
- Spring constant ($k$) = $40 \, \text{N m}^{-1}$
- Amplitude ($x_0$) = $0.1 \, \text{m}$ (This is the initial displacement from which the mass is released).

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
In SHM, the angular frequency ($\omega$) for a mass-spring system is determined by the ratio of the spring constant to the mass. We use the formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.5}} \\ \omega &= \sqrt{80} \approx 8.94 \, \text{rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(40/0.5) into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(40/0.5) into an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of (40 divided by 0.5) to find the angular frequency.

steps.title: Determine maximum speed
steps.content:
The maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$) in SHM occurs as the mass passes through the equilibrium position and is given by the product of the angular frequency and the amplitude:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega x_0$$

Using the $\omega$ we just calculated and the amplitude $x_0 = 0.1 \, \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 8.944... \times 0.1 \\ v_{\text{max}} &\approx 0.894 \, \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the first mark for correct substitution into the kinematic relationship.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the result of the previous calculation by 0.1.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the result of the square root by 0.1.

calculator.description: Multiply the previous result (8.944...) by the amplitude (0.1).

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The maximum speed of the mass during its oscillation is $0.894 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ (to three significant figures).

steps.title: Identify the conservation principle
steps.content:
In a frictionless system, the total mechanical energy is conserved. For a mass-spring system, the maximum elastic potential energy ($E_p$) at the point of release is converted entirely into maximum kinetic energy ($E_k$) when the mass passes through the equilibrium position.

This follows the principle of **Topic A.3.1 Conservation of energy**.

steps.title: Calculate maximum potential energy
steps.content:
The maximum elastic potential energy stored in the spring when it is displaced by $x_0 = 0.1 \, \text{m}$ is given by:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2}kx_0^2$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.1)^2 \\ E_p &= 20 \times 0.01 \\ E_p &= 0.2 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum elastic potential energy.

steps.title: Relate to kinetic energy
steps.content:
At the point of maximum speed ($v_{\text{max}}$), all the potential energy has been converted to kinetic energy. Using the formula for kinetic energy from the data booklet:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

We set $E_k = E_p$:

$$0.2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.5)v_{\text{max}}^2$$

steps.title: Solve for maximum speed
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for $v_{\text{max}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.2 &= 0.25 v_{\text{max}}^2 \\ v_{\text{max}}^2 &= \frac{0.2}{0.25} \\ v_{\text{max}}^2 &= 0.8 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= \sqrt{0.8} \\ v_{\text{max}} &\approx 0.894 \, \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root of (0.2 / 0.25).

calculator.description: Solve for the square root of the velocity squared.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
The maximum speed of the mass is $0.894 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for the correct substitution of values into the energy equations and **1 mark** for the final answer with correct units.

parts.content:
A pendulum of length $1.2 \, \mathrm{m}$ is swinging under the influence of gravity.

Calculate the time period of the pendulum. Take $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution into $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$:
$$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{9.8}}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$T = 2.20 \, \mathrm{s}$ (accept $2.2 \, \mathrm{s}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To calculate the time period, we first need to extract the relevant values from the question. We are given the length of the pendulum and the acceleration due to gravity.

- Length ($L$) = $1.2 \, \mathrm{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) = $9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
In **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**, we learn that the time period $T$ for a simple pendulum depends on its length and the local gravitational field. The formula from the data booklet is:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given values into the formula. This step is crucial for earning the first mark (M1) in the exam.

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{1.2}{9.8}} \end{aligned}$$

Substitute the values carefully into your calculator to find the numerical result.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the expression 2 * π * √(1.2 / 9.8). 
2. Press EXE.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [^] to get π. 
2. Type 2 * π * √(1.2 / 9.8). 
3. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type '2 * pi * sqrt(1.2 / 9.8)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time period using the pendulum formula.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
After performing the calculation, we get $T \approx 2.1986... \, \mathrm{s}$. 

Following IB conventions for significant figures (usually matching the given data or to 3 s.f.), we round this to:

$$T = 2.20 \, \mathrm{s}$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, we identify the values given in the question for the pendulum's motion. These concepts are part of **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

- Length of the pendulum, $L = 1.2 \, \mathrm{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$

steps.title: Calculate angular frequency
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum, the angular frequency $\omega$ is related to gravity and length by the following expression:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{9.8}{1.2}} \approx 2.858 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-1}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root of (9.8 divided by 1.2) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (square root), then type 9.8 / 1.2 and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the angular frequency ω.

steps.title: Calculate the time period
steps.content:
The time period $T$ is the time taken for one full oscillation. It is related to the angular frequency by the formula found in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Now, substitute the value of $\omega$ we just calculated:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{2.8577...}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 times pi by the previously calculated value of ω.

ti84.instructions: Press [2] [2nd] [^] (pi) [/] [2nd] [-] (ANS) to use the previous result, then press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the time period T using the unrounded value of ω.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
From our calculation, we find:

$$T \approx 2.20 \, \mathrm{s}$$

This matches the requirement to provide the answer to an appropriate number of significant figures (3 significant figures are standard for IB, or 2 based on the input values).

parts.content: Determine the frequency of the pendulum's oscillations.
parts.markscheme:
Substitution into $f=\frac{1}{T}$:
$$f=\frac{1}{2.20}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$f=0.455 \, \mathrm{Hz}$ (accept $0.45 \, \mathrm{Hz}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Relate frequency and period
steps.content:
Frequency ($f$) is defined as the number of oscillations occurring per second. It is mathematically related to the time period ($T$)—the time taken for one full oscillation—by the reciprocal relationship:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**. From the previous parts of the question, we know the period of the pendulum is $T = 2.20 \, \text{s}$.

steps.title: Substitute the period
steps.content:
To find the frequency, substitute the known value for the period into our equation. This substitution step is often worth the first mark in IB physics problems.

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{1}{2.20} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content: Now, perform the division to find the numerical value. We typically round our final answer to three significant figures, matching the precision of the data provided ($2.20$ is 3 sig figs).

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 / 2.20 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 2.20 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal of the period to find frequency.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives $0.4545...$ which we round to $0.455$. Always remember to include the correct unit for frequency, which is Hertz ($\text{Hz}$).

$$f = 0.455 \, \text{Hz}$$

(Note: The markscheme also accepts $0.45 \, \text{Hz}$ based on significant figure rules, but $0.455 \, \text{Hz}$ is the more precise standard.)

parts.content: Show that the angular frequency given in the equation is consistent with the physical length of the pendulum.
parts.markscheme:
Determine the theoretical angular frequency using $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Calculate $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.30}} = 5.72\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$, which matches the value provided in the equation. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given frequency
steps.content: In simple harmonic motion (SHM), the displacement equation is typically written in the form $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$, where $\omega$ represents the angular frequency. Looking at the provided equation, we can identify the value for $\omega$ directly.

steps.title: State the theoretical formula
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum oscillating with small amplitudes, the angular frequency $\omega$ is determined by the local acceleration due to gravity $g$ and the length of the pendulum $L$. 

This relationship is derived from the period formula for a pendulum, $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$. Since $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$, the theoretical angular frequency is:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

This is a fundamental relationship for oscillatory systems as described in **Topic C.1: Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Calculate the angular frequency
steps.content:
We now substitute the known values into our formula. We use the standard value for Earth's gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or $9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$ as per the constants list), and the given length $L = 0.30\text{ m}$.

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.30}}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and divide 9.81 by 0.30.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of (g/L) to find the angular frequency.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Evaluating the calculation gives:

$$\omega \approx 5.72\text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

Since this calculated theoretical value matches the coefficient of $t$ in the displacement equation ($5.72$), the given equation is consistent with the physical length of the pendulum. 

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark (**M1**) for identifying/using the formula $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$ and a second mark (**A1**) for the final calculation that shows the match.

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
To start, we need to extract the experimental angular frequency ($\omega$) from the displacement equation provided in the question. 

The general equation for simple harmonic motion is:
$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$$

By comparing this to the given equation, we can see that the coefficient inside the cosine function represents the angular frequency.

steps.title: Use the pendulum formula
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum undergoing small oscillations, the angular frequency is determined by the acceleration due to gravity ($g$) and the length of the pendulum ($L$). This relationship is found in **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion** and is derived from the period formula:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for length
steps.content:
Using the **Reverse Calculation of Length** approach, we want to see if the $\omega$ from the equation results in the correct physical length. First, we rearrange the formula to isolate $L$:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega^2 &= \frac{g}{L} \\ L &= \frac{g}{\omega^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with calculation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values: $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (the standard value used in IB markschemes) and $\omega = 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$.

$$L = \frac{9.81}{(5.72)^2}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division of 9.81 by the square of 5.72.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the physical length based on the provided angular frequency.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
The calculation yields:
$$L \approx 0.30 \text{ m}$$

This matches the physical length given in the problem specification exactly. Therefore, the angular frequency in the equation is consistent with the pendulum's length.

**Marking Note:** 
- **M1:** For correctly identifying or rearranging the $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$ relationship.
- **A1:** For showing that the calculated value (0.30 m) matches the physical data provided.

steps.title: Identify the experimental angular frequency
steps.content:
From the given displacement equation $x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$, we can identify the angular frequency $\omega$ by comparing it to the standard SHM equation $x = A \cos(\omega t)$.

$$\omega = 5.72\text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate period from the equation
steps.content:
We can determine the time period ($T$) of the oscillation using the relationship between period and angular frequency:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

Substituting our value for $\omega$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{5.72} \approx 1.098\text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi / 5.72 and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the period from the given angular frequency.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical period
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the theoretical period of a pendulum using its physical properties. For small amplitudes, the period depends on the length $L$ and the acceleration due to gravity $g$.

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

Using $L = 0.30\text{ m}$ and $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{0.30}{9.81}} \approx 1.099\text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * pi * sqrt(0.30 / 9.81) and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type 2pi * sqrt(0.30 / 9.81) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the theoretical period of the pendulum.

steps.title: Compare results and conclude
steps.content:
Comparing the two results:
- Period from equation: $T \approx 1.10\text{ s}$
- Period from physical length: $T \approx 1.10\text{ s}$

Since both values yield the same time period, the angular frequency $\omega = 5.72\text{ rad s}^{-1}$ is consistent with the physical length of the pendulum.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the pendulum bob, assuming a mass of $0.40\ \text{kg}$.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the maximum speed: $v_{\text{max}} = \omega A = 5.72 \times 0.050 = 0.286\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
Calculate the kinetic energy: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_{\text{max}}^2 = 0.5 \times 0.40 \times (0.286)^2 = 0.0164\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To find the maximum kinetic energy, we first need to identify the characteristics of the motion from the displacement equation provided. Simple harmonic motion (SHM) follows the general form $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$, where $A$ is the amplitude and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.

From the equation $x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$, we can identify:
- Amplitude $A = 0.050\ \text{m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5.72\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate maximum velocity
steps.content:
The maximum velocity ($v_{\text{max}}$) in SHM occurs when the object passes through the equilibrium position. It is calculated using the formula:

$$v_{\text{max}} = \omega A$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{max}} &= 5.72 \times 0.050 \\ v_{\text{max}} &= 0.286\ \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum velocity by multiplying angular frequency and amplitude.

steps.title: Determine maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now we can find the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k, \text{max}}$) using the mass of the bob and the maximum velocity. From the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet, we use the kinetic energy formula:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} mv^{2}$$

Substitute the mass $m = 0.40\ \text{kg}$ and $v_{\text{max}} = 0.286\ \text{m s}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k, \text{max}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.40 \times (0.286)^{2} \\ E_{k, \text{max}} &= 0.2 \times 0.081796 \\ E_{k, \text{max}} &\approx 0.0164\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the kinetic energy using the mass and the squared velocity.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The maximum kinetic energy of the pendulum bob is $0.0164\ \text{J}$.

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for correctly calculating the maximum velocity ($0.286\ \text{m s}^{-1}$) and **1 mark** for the final kinetic energy value with appropriate units.

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
To calculate the energy, we first need to extract the amplitude ($A$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general form of a displacement equation for simple harmonic motion is:
$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$$

Comparing this to the equation provided in the question:
$$x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$$

We can see that:
- Amplitude $A = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Recall the SHM energy formula
steps.content:
The maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k,\text{max}}$) of an object in simple harmonic motion occurs at the equilibrium position, where it is equal to the total mechanical energy ($E_T$) of the system.

The formula for the total energy in SHM is:
$$E = \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2$$

This formula is derived from the standard kinetic energy formula $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ by substituting the maximum velocity $v_{\text{max}} = \omega A$.

steps.title: Calculate maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into the energy formula:
- Mass $m = 0.40 \text{ kg}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
- Amplitude $A = 0.050 \text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,\text{max}} &= \frac{1}{2} (0.40) (5.72)^2 (0.050)^2 \\ E_{k,\text{max}} &= 0.20 \times 32.7184 \times 0.0025 \\ E_{k,\text{max}} &= 0.0163592 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $0.0164 \text{ J}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into an empty cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy using the formula $E = 0.5 \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot A^2$.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
The maximum kinetic energy of the pendulum bob is $0.0164 \text{ J}$.

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark for correctly identifying the maximum speed (or using the correct energy relationship) and one mark for the final numerical answer with units.

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To calculate the maximum kinetic energy using the potential energy approach, we first extract the necessary values from the problem:
- Mass of the bob, $m = 0.40\text{ kg}$
- Length of the pendulum, $L = 0.30\text{ m}$
- Amplitude of motion, $A = 0.050\text{ m}$ (from the equation $x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$)

steps.title: Calculate maximum displacement angle
steps.content:
The amplitude $A$ represents the maximum horizontal displacement. We can find the maximum angle of displacement, $\theta$, using trigonometry:

$$\sin \theta = \frac{A}{L}$$

$$\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{0.050}{0.30}\right) \approx 0.1674 \text{ rad}$$

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to Radians. Enter asin(0.05/0.3).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in RADIAN mode. Press [2nd] [SIN] (0.050 / 0.30) [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the maximum angle theta in radians.

steps.title: Determine vertical height change
steps.content:
When the pendulum swings to its maximum angle, it rises by a vertical height $h$. This height is the difference between the full length $L$ and the vertical component of the length at the maximum angle:

$$h = L - L\cos\theta = L(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Substituting our values:
$$h = 0.30 \times (1 - \cos(0.1674)) \approx 0.004196 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical height h.

steps.title: Calculate maximum kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the Law of Conservation of Energy, the maximum kinetic energy ($E_{k,\text{max}}$) at the bottom of the swing is equal to the maximum gravitational potential energy ($\Delta E_p$) at the highest point:

$$\Delta E_p = mg\Delta h$$

Using $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ from the data booklet:
$$E_{k,\text{max}} = 0.40 \times 9.8 \times 0.004196$$
$$E_{k,\text{max}} \approx 0.0164 \text{ J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy value.

parts.content: Determine the speed of the bob when its displacement is $0.030\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
Substitute values into the speed-displacement relation: $v = 5.72 \times \sqrt{0.050^2 - 0.030^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Obtain the speed: $v = 5.72 \times 0.040 = 0.229\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Extract SHM parameters
steps.content:
To find the speed at a specific displacement, we first need to identify the amplitude ($x_0$) and the angular frequency ($\omega$) from the given displacement equation. 

The general equation for SHM displacement is:
$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

By comparing this to the provided equation:
$$x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$$

We can see that:
- Amplitude $x_0 = 0.050\ \text{m}$
- Angular frequency $\omega = 5.72\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Apply speed-displacement formula
steps.content:
In **Topic C.1 Simple Harmonic Motion**, we learn that the speed $v$ of an oscillator at any displacement $x$ is related to its amplitude and angular frequency by the formula:

$$v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$$

This formula allows us to calculate the speed directly without needing to find the specific time $t$ when the displacement is $0.030\ \text{m}$.

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the equation to find the speed when the displacement is $0.030\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 5.72 \times \sqrt{0.050^2 - 0.030^2} \\ v &= 5.72 \times \sqrt{0.0025 - 0.0009} \\ v &= 5.72 \times \sqrt{0.0016} \\ v &= 5.72 \times 0.040 \\ v &= 0.2288 \\ v &\approx 0.229\ \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation would earn you the substitution mark (**M1**) and the final answer mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Type the formula into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input the calculation into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the speed using the SHM speed-displacement relation.

steps.title: Identify SHM parameters
steps.content:
From the given displacement equation $x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$, we can identify the amplitude $x_0$ and the angular frequency $\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= 0.050 \text{ m} \\ \omega &= 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This question asks for the speed when $x = 0.030 \text{ m}$. We will find the time $t$ when this occurs and then use the velocity function.

steps.title: Solve for the time
steps.content:
We set the displacement equation equal to $0.030 \text{ m}$ to find the corresponding time $t$:

$$0.030 = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$$

Rearranging to solve for the cosine term:

$$\cos(5.72t) = \frac{0.030}{0.050} = 0.6$$

To find the time, we take the inverse cosine:

$$5.72t = \arccos(0.6)$$

$$t = \frac{\arccos(0.6)}{5.72}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression acos(0.6) divided by 5.72.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the value of t.

calculator.description: Calculate the time $t$ when the displacement is $0.030\text{ m}$. Ensure your calculator is in RADIAN mode.

steps.title: Apply velocity function
steps.content:
The velocity $v(t)$ is the derivative of displacement $x(t)$. For $x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$, the velocity is:

$$v(t) = -x_0 \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

Substituting our values $x_0 = 0.050$ and $\omega = 5.72$:

$$v = -0.050 \times 5.72 \times \sin(5.72t)$$

Since we need the **speed**, we take the absolute value (magnitude) of the velocity.

steps.title: Calculate the final speed
steps.content:
Using the value of $t$ found in Step 1, we calculate the speed:

$$\text{speed} = | -0.050 \times 5.72 \times \sin(5.72 \times 0.162...) |$$

Alternatively, since $\cos(5.72t) = 0.6$, we can use the identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ to find $\sin(5.72t) = \sqrt{1 - 0.6^2} = 0.8$. 

$$\text{speed} = 0.050 \times 5.72 \times 0.8$$

$$\text{speed} = 0.2288 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

desmos.instructions: Multiply the values to find the speed.

calculator.description: Calculate the final speed using the time $t$ or the trigonometric identity result.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures as provided in the markscheme:

$$\text{speed} = 0.229 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This method matches the result obtained using the speed-displacement formula $v = \omega \sqrt{x_0^2 - x^2}$ from the markscheme.

parts.content: Calculate the magnitude of the acceleration of the pendulum bob at this displacement.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate the acceleration magnitude using $a = \omega^2 x = (5.72)^2 \times 0.030 = 0.982\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify angular frequency
steps.content:
To find the acceleration, we first need to identify the angular frequency $\omega$ from the displacement equation. The general form for simple harmonic motion is:

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

By comparing this to the given equation, we can see that $\omega$ is the coefficient of $t$.

steps.title: Extract the value of ω
steps.content:
From the equation $x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$, we identify:

$$\omega = 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Use SHM defining equation
steps.content:
The defining characteristic of simple harmonic motion is that the acceleration $a$ is proportional to the displacement $x$, according to the formula:

$$a = -\omega^2 x$$

Since the question asks for the **magnitude** of the acceleration, we can ignore the negative sign (which just indicates direction) and use:

$$|a| = \omega^2 x$$

steps.title: Calculate the acceleration magnitude
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the angular frequency $\omega = 5.72 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$ and the displacement $x = 0.030 \text{ m}$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} a &= (5.72)^2 \times 0.030 \\ a &= 32.7184 \times 0.030 \\ a &\approx 0.982 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation matches the requirement for the mark.

gdc.instructions: Enter 5.72^2 * 0.030 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of acceleration using the squared angular frequency and displacement.

steps.title: Identify the displacement function
steps.content:
To find the acceleration using calculus, we start with the given displacement function. This function describes how the position of the pendulum bob changes over time. 

$$x(t) = 0.050 \cos(5.72t)$$

In this equation, the angular frequency $\omega$ is the coefficient of $t$.

steps.title: Differentiate to find velocity
steps.content:
Velocity $v(t)$ is the first derivative of displacement with respect to time ($v = \frac{dx}{dt}$). Using the chain rule for the cosine function:

$$v(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[0.050 \cos(5.72t)]$$
$$v(t) = -0.050 \times 5.72 \sin(5.72t)$$

This gives us the velocity of the bob at any time $t$.

steps.title: Differentiate to find acceleration
steps.content:
Acceleration $a(t)$ is the derivative of velocity with respect to time ($a = \frac{dv}{dt}$). Differentiating again:

$$a(t) = \frac{d}{dt}[-0.050 \times 5.72 \sin(5.72t)]$$
$$a(t) = -0.050 \times (5.72)^2 \cos(5.72t)$$

This confirms the defining characteristic of Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM): acceleration is proportional to displacement but in the opposite direction.

steps.title: Relate acceleration to displacement
steps.content:
Notice that the term $0.050 \cos(5.72t)$ is exactly our original displacement function $x(t)$. We can rewrite the acceleration as:

$$a = -(5.72)^2 x$$

To find the **magnitude** of the acceleration, we ignore the negative sign (which just indicates direction) and use the absolute value:

$$|a| = \omega^2 x$$

steps.title: Calculate the magnitude
steps.content:
Using the displacement value $x = 0.030\text{ m}$ (provided in the context of the problem) and the angular frequency $\omega = 5.72\text{ rad s}^{-1}$:

$$a = (5.72)^2 \times 0.030$$

$$a \approx 0.982\text{ m s}^{-2}$$

gdc.instructions: Input (5.72)^2 * 0.030 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression (5.72)^2 * 0.030 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the product of the square of 5.72 and 0.030.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of acceleration using the squared angular frequency and displacement.

steps.title: Identify the dynamics relationship
steps.content:
For a simple pendulum oscillating at small angles, the acceleration $a$ is proportional to the displacement $x$ from the equilibrium position. We can use the specific pendulum dynamics relationship:

$$a = \frac{g}{L}x$$

This comes from the restoring force in a pendulum, where for small angles, the motion is simple harmonic. This relates to **Topic C.1 Simple harmonic motion**.

steps.title: Identify the known variables
steps.content:
From the question and the data booklet, we have the following values:
- Acceleration due to gravity: $$g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$
- Length of the pendulum: $$L = 0.30 \text{ m}$$
- Displacement: $$x = 0.030 \text{ m}$$ (as indicated by the calculation in the markscheme for this specific part).

steps.title: Calculate the acceleration magnitude
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into our relationship to find the magnitude of the acceleration:

$$\begin{aligned} a &= \frac{9.8}{0.30} \times 0.030 \\ a &= 0.98 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the values into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of acceleration using the pendulum relationship.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The magnitude of the acceleration of the pendulum bob at this displacement is $$0.98 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$. 

Note: This matches the result obtained using the angular frequency method ($a = \omega^2 x$), as $\omega^2 \approx \frac{g}{L}$ for this pendulum.

options.markscheme: This represents only the gravitational force, not the net force acting on the oscillating mass.

In simple harmonic motion, the net (restoring) force is proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position and acts in the opposite direction. For a vertical spring-mass system, the net force is the sum of the spring force and the weight. At equilibrium, $ke = mg$ (where $e$ is the extension). At a displacement $y$ above equilibrium, the net force is $F = k(e - y) - mg = ke - ky - mg = -ky$.
This expression lacks the negative sign required for a restoring force, which must act in the direction opposite to the displacement.
This expression does not correctly account for the cancellation of the weight by the equilibrium tension in the spring.

The table below describes the mass of the block and the spring constant for two horizontal simple harmonic oscillators, $X$ and $Y$, which undergo oscillation on a frictionless surface.

| System | Mass of block | Spring constant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $X$ | $m$ | $k$ |
| $Y$ | $4m$ | $2k$ |

What is the ratio of the frequency of system $X$ to the frequency of system $Y$ ($\frac{f_X}{f_Y}$)?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.1 Simple harmonic motion > C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.1.2 Time period of oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the ratio of frequencies of two mass–spring SHM systems given m and k values, using the time period/frequency relation f = (1/2π)√(k/m).

options.markscheme: This would be the result if the frequency were inversely proportional to the ratio of the mass and spring constant without the square root, effectively calculating $\frac{2k/4m}{k/m}$.
This represents the inverse ratio $\frac{f_Y}{f_X}$ rather than $\frac{f_X}{f_Y}$.

The frequency of a mass-spring system is given by $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. For system $X$, $f_X \propto \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$. For system $Y$, $f_Y \propto \sqrt{\frac{2k}{4m}} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{2m}}$. Therefore, the ratio $\frac{f_X}{f_Y} = \frac{\sqrt{k/m}}{\sqrt{k/2m}} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m} \cdot \frac{2m}{k}} = \sqrt{2}$.
This result occurs if the student neglects the square root in the frequency formula, calculating $\frac{k/m}{2k/4m} = 2$.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the value of gravitational acceleration $(g)$ using a simple pendulum and a free-fall experiment. The following equation relates the period of a pendulum $(T)$ to its length $(L)$ and gravitational acceleration:

$$
T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}
$$

For the free-fall experiment, the displacement $(s)$ of an object dropped from rest is related to $g$ and time $(t)$ using the equation:

$$
s=\frac{1}{2} g t^2
$$

The following data was collected for both experiments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Pendulum Experiment }\\
&\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline \text { Length of Pendulum }(L) / \mathrm{m} & \text { Measured Period }(T) / \mathrm{s} \\
\hline 0.50 & 1.42 \\
\hline 1.00 & 2.01 \\
\hline 1.50 & 2.45 \\
\hline 2.00 & 2.83 \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Free-Fall Experiment }\\
&\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline \text { Drop Height }(s) / \mathrm{m} & \text { Time }(t) / \mathrm{s} \\
\hline 1.0 & 0.45 \\
\hline 2.0 & 0.64 \\
\hline 3.0 & 0.78 \\
\hline 4.0 & 0.90 \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$
